Document 400 of 453

MPs hike our taxes and stuff their own wallets

Author: Ctf Franco Terrazzano

Publication info: Ctf Franco Terrazzano. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mps-hike-our-taxes-stuff-their-own-wallets/docview/2938143805/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=MPs+hike+our+taxes+and+stuff+their+own+wallets&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-03-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Ctf+Franco+Terrazzano&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: On the same day members of Parliament take more money out of your wallet, they'll be stuffing more into their own.
On April 1, the federal government is hiking carbon and alcohol taxes. That very same day, MPs will take another pay raise.
A backbench MP's salary is currently $194,600.
A minister collects $287,400, while the prime minister takes home a $389,200 annual salary.
This year's estimated pay raise will range from an extra $8,100 for a backbencher to $16,200 for PM Justin Trudeau, based on contract data published by the government.
But this year's raise is just the tip of the iceberg.
In fact, this year's raise will be MPs'fifth pay raise since the beginning of 2020.
A pandemic, lockdowns, job and business losses and their constituents not being able to afford ground beef hasn't stopped MPs from taking raises every year.
As of this April, MPs will receive an annual salary about $23,800 higher than they did pre-pandemic, while Trudeau will take home an extra $47,600.
The government could stop the upcoming pay raise like it did between 2010 and '13 in response to the recession.
But so far, the silence from all political parties on this year's pay raise has been deafening.
Politicians don't deserve raises when they make life more expensive with tax hikes.
After the April hike, the carbon tax will cost 17 cents per litre of gasoline, 21 cents per litre of diesel and 15 cents per cubic metre of natural gas.
That carbon tax will cost the average family up to $911 a year more than they get back rebates, according to the parliamentary budget officer.
While the feds are busy raising taxes, provincial governments of all political stripes have provided taxpayers with relief.
The New Democrats in Manitoba suspended their 14-cent-perlitre gas tax.
The Liberals in Newfoundland and Labrador cut their gas tax by 8 cents per litre. Conservative governments in Alberta and Ontario also provided fuel tax relief.
You could be forgiven if the upcoming MP pay raise and carbon tax hike drive you to drink. But there's more bad news: Trudeau will reach deeper into your pocket whenever you go to the liquor store or bar.
Taxes already make up more than half the price Canadians pay for alcohol.
In fact, Canadians pay five times more tax on a case of beer than our friends south of the border. In Saskatchewan, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland and Labrador, the tax on a case of beer costs more than the total price of a case in half of American states.
And on April 1, Trudeau is hiking his federal taxes on alcohol by another 4.7%.
Even worse, Trudeau made the alcohol tax hike automatic so MPs won't even vote on it.
Last year, the feds capped their alcohol tax increase at 2%. Trudeau shouldn't have hiked the tax at all, but the smaller increase reduced the tax burden by $100 million. The least the federal government could do this year is freeze its alcohol taxes.
Politicians shouldn't be giving themselves a raise the same day they take more money from tapped-out taxpayers. Politicians shouldn't take more money from people already lined up at food banks. And they don't deserve a penny more.
All politicians from every political party should oppose April's tax and pay hike.
Terrazzano is federal director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Subject: Raises; Alcohol; Members of Parliament; Politics; Carbon; Gasoline taxes; Politicians; Pandemics; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Raises Gasoline taxes Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Canada; Newfoundland & Labrador Canada

Identifier / keyword: members; parliament; money; wallet; stuffing; april

Document 401 of 453

Mulroney a brave man with a bold vision

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-brave-man-with-bold-vision/docview/2933856523/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+a+brave+man+with+a+bold+vision&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.16&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney was a courageous leader, unafraid to make tough decisions. He was a steadfast warrior on the front lines of human rights. And he was a visionary who did more than any other prime minister to make a real and lasting impact on the environment.
Mulroney died Thursday at age 84. Born to a large family in a Quebec steel town, Mulroney put himself through law school and joined the business world, but he never forgot his blue-collar roots.
First elected in a byelection in 1983, the following year he swept the Progressive Conservatives to their largest-ever majority government.
Mulroney negotiated this country's first free trade agreement with the U.S. and Mexico, over strenuous opposition from detractors. It created jobs and prosperity and formed the lasting strength of Canada's economy. Yes, he brought in the GST, an unpopular tax. Successive governments have silently thanked him for it. It was the right thing to do.
On the environment, he's best remembered for his visionary pact with the U.S. on acid rain. He also brought in the Environmental Protection Act and created eight new national parks.
He built close relationships with world leaders, especially U.S. presidents Ronald Reagan and George H.W. Bush.
And he was widely respected for standing up for human rights in South Africa and leading the push to free Nelson Mandela. Mulroney refused to be pushed around by former British prime minister Margaret Thatcher - the Iron Lady - at the 1987 Commonwealth Conference in Vancouver. He insisted the Commonwealth support sanctions against South Africa and its racist apartheid policies. Canada, he said, would be on the "right side of history" in ending apartheid.
Mandela never forgot Mulroney's principled stand. It's why Canada was the first country he visited after 27 years in prison.
Mulroney had setbacks, of course. All politicians do. They're well documented and now's not the time to rake over old foibles.
In his latter years, he became this country's elder statesman. Aspiring leaders sought his advice and mentorship.
We should remember Mulroney as a brave Canadian who changed this country for the better. We should celebrate him as a man who loved his country and did what was right, not necessarily what was popular.
May he rest in peace.

Subject: Apartheid; Environmental impact; Human rights; Prime ministers

Location: United States--US; South Africa; Canada

Identifier / keyword: brian; mulroney; courageous; leader; unafraid; tough

Title: Mulroney a brave man with a bold vision

Document 402 of 453

Thousands without care: Stiles; NDP leader rips provincial health-care funding

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/thousands-without-care-stiles-ndp-leader-rips/docview/2933856516/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Thousands+without+care%3A+Stiles%3B+NDP+leader+rips+provincial+health-care+funding&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-03-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ontario NDP Leader Marit Stiles is warning that up to 10,000 Perth and area residents are at risk of losing access to primary care because of a provincial funding decision.
Two large primary-care teams in the town applied for funding under a provincial program aimed at setting up inter-professional health teams, enabling clinics to care for more patients.
Just one of those teams received funding under the program, putting the other clinic, the Tay River Health Centre, at risk of losing doctors and being unable to provide care for patients.
Doctors, patients and town officials have urged the province to reconsider its decision, saying it will destabilize health care in the region, drive health professionals away and leave residents with unequal access to primary care.
"Ten thousand people in the Perth area are at risk of losing primary care because Ford's Conser vatives decided not to fund one of their local clinics," she said.
Stiles added that the Tay River Health Centre submitted an application under the program for $2.9 million to cover 8,500 patients in addition to those it already cared for.
It did not receive funding. "Perth already has a dire shortage of primary-care providers, and without additional funding the Tay River Health Centre may be forced to close its doors within three years," she said.
Stiles called for "urgent action" to ensure primary care is available for everyone in Ontario, especially rural areas of the province.
A spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the province received an overwhelming number of applications and worked hard to approve those that could connect people to care immediately.
Jones announced $90 million in funding for teams across Ontario.
Spokesperson Hannah Jensen said unsuccessful applicants were encouraged to "work with their Ontario Health region and the Ministry of Health on innovative action they can take to make it easier to access care in their community."
"We think it is a gross oversight," said Dr. Taylor Ferrier, medical director of the Tay River Health Centre. "All patients in Perth deserve this level of funding and support."
About 2.3 million Ontario residents are currently without family doctors.
That number is expected to double in the next few years. epayne@postmedia.com

Document 403 of 453

Unsurpassed; Architect of federal Tories' '84 landslide dead at 84

Author: Riga, Andy

Publication info: Riga, Andy. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/unsurpassed-architect-federal-tories-84-landslide/docview/2933665820/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Unsurpassed%3B+Architect+of+federal+Tories%27+%2784+landslide+dead+at+84&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Riga%2C+Andy&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: MONTREAL - Brian Mulroney, whose tenure as prime minister was marked by momentous economic change and two tumultuous attempts to reform the Canadian Constitution, has died at age 84, his daughter Caroline Mulroney announced Thursday evening.
"He died peacefully, surrounded by family," Caroline Mulroney announced on X, formerly Twitter, just before 6 p.m. "We will share details of arrangements when they become available."
In his time in power from 1984 to 1993, Mulroney introduced the GST and negotiated free-trade agreements with the United States and Mexico. Contentious at the time, the GST and free trade are now mainstays of the Canadian economy.
But Mulroney, a Quebec-born anglophone embraced as family by francophone Quebecers, saw one of his biggest ambitions as PM and leader of the Progressive Conservative party - to convince his home province to endorse the Canadian Constitution - end in utter failure.
Instead of unifying the country, the efforts almost led to its destruction, fuelled by the defection of Lucien Bouchard, a minister in Mulroney's government and his onetime confidant, who almost split Quebec from the rest of Canada.
By the time he quit, Mulroney was deeply unpopular. In his aftermath, the Progressive Conservatives were left in tatters.
Born of modest means, Mulroney grew up in Baie-Comeau, Que., on the north shore of the St. Lawrence River, his father an electrician at the local paper mill, his mother a homemaker.
He took to politics naturally as a teen at St. Francis Xavier University in Nova Scotia, joining the Tory club and attending the convention that elected John Diefenbaker as party leader in 1956.
His sights already set on a political career, Mulroney went to Universite Laval in Quebec City for his law degree.
There, he connected with francophones and improved his French. Fellow student Bouchard, a bookish nationalist, became a close friend.
He took a bold stab at federal politics. Instead of starting as a backbencher, he aimed for the top, running for the Progressive Conservative leadership in 1976 against Joe Clark and Claude Wagner.
Clark won the day, but a few years later Mulroney would have his chance. Playing up his Quebec roots and business know-how, Mulroney forged a big-tent coalition, bringing together Quebec nationalists and conservatives of all stripes.
It worked like a charm. In 1984, Mulroney won one of the biggest landslides in Canadian history. His PCs took 211 of 282 seats in the House of Commons, including more than three-quarters of the seats in Quebec.
Mulroney has been praised for signing a pact with the U.S. on acid rain and for helping bring about the Montreal Protocol, a global agreement to phase out ozone-depleting substances. He also resisted calls to lift sanctions against South Africa over apartheid.
But his time in office was also marred by multiple scandals - 10 cabinet ministers left amid controversy - as well as criticism over patronage favours for Tory faithful.
Mulroney is survived by his wife Mila and his children Ben, Caroline, Mark and Nicolas.

Subject: Leadership; Francophones

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: United States--US; Canada; Quebec Canada

Document 404 of 453

Reformer in prime, legend in own time; PM didn't just win big, he made the world better

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reformer-prime-legend-own-time-pm-didnt-just-win/docview/2933665787/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reformer+in+prime%2C+legend+in+own+time%3B+PM+didn%27t+just+win+big%2C+he+made+the+world+better&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister, who served from 1984 until 1993, died on Thursday. His daughter Caroline, a cabinet minister in the Ontario government of Doug Ford, made the announcement via social media.
"On behalf of my mother and our family, it is with great sadness we announce the passing of my father, the Right Honourable Brian Mulroney, Canada's 18th prime minister. He died peacefully, surrounded by family," she posted just before 6 p.m.
Mulroney was 84 and just three weeks away from his 85th birthday on March 20.
"He's a legend!" Mulroney's son Mark was fond of saying about his father, and he was right. This wasn't just a son looking up to his father, and anyone who has spoken to any of Mulroney's children has heard their deep admiration.
Mark's statement was on his dad's legacy, including winning back-to-back majorities for the Conservatives, something that hadn't been done since Sir. John A. Macdonald.
In 1984, Mulroney had barely been leader of the Progressive Conservatives for a year when he took on the Liberal juggernaut and won.
Mulroney and his team took 211 seats with just over 50% of the vote. No prime minister has won as many seats as Mulroney since.
On the policy front, this is the man who brought in a Canada-U.S. free trade deal that improved Canadian prosperity since it came in. He brought in the goods and services tax, replacing hidden taxes that could be manipulated, because he knew the GST was the right thing to do, even if unpopular.
If it weren't for the GST, Jean Chretien wouldn't have been able to balance the budgets the way he did after taking over. Mulroney's decision to implement the GST and to tackle problematic spending inside the federal government, including reducing the deficit from 8% of GDP to 5%, paved the way for Chretien to tackle the fiscal mess that had started under Pierre Trudeau.
Those on their own, all signature achievements, would mark a distinguished career, but Mulroney had more to offer.
He privatized Petro-Canada, Air Canada as well as de Havilland Air and Canadair. It's difficult for most Canadians to fathom the government owning a gas station, an airline and two aircraft manufacturers, but when Mulroney came to office, there were 61 Crown corporations. When he left there were just 38.
He tried, though ultimately failed, to find a way to get Quebec to sign the constitution. He first offered up the Meech Lake accord and then the Charlottetown accord.
Mulroney was about taking risks though, because the only way you can make great changes is by taking risks.
He won on free trade. He won on the GST. His risk on taming the finances paved the way for others - including Canadian taxpayers to win. As my colleague Warren Kinsella has beautifully written, Mulroney won on South Africa as well.
He led the charge against powerful world leaders including then-U.S. president Ronald Reagan and British PM Margaret Thatcher in opposing the apartheid regime in South Africa. Mulroney's push for economic sanctions and political isolation eventually won the day and true freedom for the people of that country.
Despite going against Reagan and Thatcher, Mulroney was well regarded by them and other world leaders and often sought out for advice after he left office. He was an invited speaker, and asked to give eulogies at the funerals of Reagan and his successor George H.W. Bush.
To sit and listen to Mulroney speak was a true treat. The man had a gift with words. It was like his entire body had kissed the Blarney Stone.
In 2022, I had the chance to listen to Mulroney speak at an event at a Toronto hotel ballroom. I'd covered the man before, but this was different.
Even at 82, he held the audience in the palm of his hand with his stories. He had a glint in his eye and a spring in his step and from my conversation with him, he was still paying close attention to politics.
Much has been made that he made nice statements about Justin Trudeau. Less known is that he had Pierre Poilievre over to his house for dinner and was thoroughly impressed.
Let the other outlets and writers use Mulroney's passing to go over his sins, something they will revel in. I come here to praise Mulroney, not to bury him. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Free trade; GST; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade GST

Document 405 of 453

Mulroney took big risks on big issues - and history would prove him right

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/mulroney-took-big-risks-on-issues-history-would/docview/2933665715/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Mulroney+took+big+risks+on+big+issues+-+and+history+would+prove+him+right&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-03-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The biggest achievements in politics - the only achievements, really - are the ones involving risk, as in taking a risk, making a decision, making a statement, making a law that entails risk to you and your career.
Brian Mulroney took risks. I didn't work for him. In fact, I worked for Jean Chretien, his Liberal party opponent, and part of my job was to make the Mulroney government miserable.
Despite that - and when behind closed doors - Martin Brian Mulroney, PC, CC, GOQ, was a bit of a marvel to us because he took risks, because he had guts.
Case in point, South Africa. In the '80s, when Mulroney was prime minister - and presiding over two successive super-majorities - South Africa still practised apartheid. Apartheid was institutionalized racism, essentially. It was racial segregation and discrimination that had been forced on the black majority in South Africa by a white minority.
Ronald Reagan and Margaret Thatcher were Brian Mulroney's closest allies internationally. They professed to oppose apartheid, but they vociferously opposed international sanctions to bring it to an end. Thatcher called them "counterproductive."
Brian Mulroney stood up to Reagan and Thatcher, and many within his own Conservative party. In September 1986, Mulroney imposed tough sanctions on the apartheid regime and encouraged other nations to do likewise.
Said he: "I viewed apartheid with the same degree of disgust that I attached to the Nazis - the authors of the most odious offence in modern history."
Nelson Mandela thanked him for that, saying Mulroney and Canada would be forever remembered for their support.
Mulroney's other great and courageous achievement: free trade.
And, yes, we Liberals initially opposed it - or, at least, the John Turner-era Grits did (Chretien, as the country would soon see, not so much).
But Brian Mulroney saw where the world was heading - with technology ushering in an era of lightning-fast global commerce, dominated by companies all too willing to move to where they could do more business for less.
Mulroney's free trade stance was targeted by Turner during the 1988 federal election - and, for a while, it very nearly turned the tide against the Tory leader. He could have blinked, then, and backed away.
He could have reversed himself. He didn't. Mulroney persisted - and won another huge majority, and signed a comprehensive free trade deal with the United States.
He had a heart, too
There were other, less notable, parts to the man.
On the Hill, in the pre-Twitter days - when things were more civilized - all of us heard stories about Brian Mulroney's human side, like a gift of ties to Brian Tobin, his Liberal tormentor, when the MP's son was born; a call to Chretien during a health scare; quiet wishes whenever a Liberal was going through personal hardship. Not for publication, ever - but never forgotten by the recipients.
Brian Mulroney was not a great politician and prime minister because he won two big elections. He was one of the great ones because he took risks - because he took risks with things that mattered, the things that will be remembered by history.
My deepest condolences to his family, some of whom I now know and consider good friends.
Your dad was a great one. He will be missed.

Subject: Apartheid; Free trade; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Free trade

Location: South Africa

People: Mulroney, Brian

YOU SAID IT: What about Ford?

Author: Menary, Doug

Publication info: Menary, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-said-what-about-ford/docview/2933354956/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=YOU+SAID+IT%3A+What+about+Ford%3F&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Menary%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WHAT ABOUT FORD?
Is the Sun newspaper anti-Liberal in the letters to the editor it publishes?
While the constant Justin Trudeau bashing may well be deserved, it is rare to see the same negative letters about the Doug Ford government.
The health-care system in general and the doctor shortage in particular need much more focus, in my opinion. It is estimated that, within the next couple of years, 25 per cent of us in Ontario will be without a family doctor. This is not acceptable.
What concrete plans does the Ford government have to resolve this crisis? Nothing that leaves me feeling comfortable.
DON DALY
NEPEAN
GO WEST, YOUNG MAN
Go west, young man. Make sure you settle before you reach British Columbia.
It's looking like Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta are young Canadians' best choices for opportunities, affordability and a decent future if they plan to stay in Canada. Growth there seems inevitable as Ontario has infrastructure problems because of poor immigration policies. Quebec doesn't want you unless you are francophone. Nova Scotia, New Brunswick, Newfoundland and Labrador, and Prince Edward Island have limited resources outside of fisheries, and there is a growing worry from weather extremes for ambitious youth to move in that direction. There is the option of Canada's North, but the neglect by governments to provide the necessities of life (like clean drinking water) takes this option out.
Young Canadians had the option of a military service career, but governments' neglect of our military has taken that career option away for most.
Go West, young man, seems like a good option. Make Canada great again. Spread the power.
MIKE DEFALCO
ARNPRIOR
WHAT A HYPOCRITE
Talk about a hypocrite; Justin Trudeau claimed that Pierre Poilievere is driving political wedges and sowing division with his belief that biological males have no place in sports or change rooms labelled for females.The PM most likely to drive political wedges and sow division has been Trudeau since taking office. This hypocrite has divided more friends and family, etc., with all his dictatorial policies and actions from just COVID alone, not to mention all the other problems he's created. The next federal election can't come soon enough.
RENE KLABOUCH
KEMPTVILLE

Document 407 of 453

Pharmacare plan short on detail, big on cost

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pharmacare-plan-short-on-detail-big-cost/docview/2933291583/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Pharmacare+plan+short+on+detail%2C+big+on+cost&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Before we fire up the confetti cannon to celebrate the federal plan for Pharmacare, we need to see some details - and a price tag.
The Alberta government has said it will opt out of the drug plan and wants the federal government to give the province its share of the money with no strings attached.
Quebec has also indicated it will opt out. Ontario wants to see more details before it endorses the plan.
Pharmacare is part of the costly supplyand-confidence agreement between New Democratic Party Leader Jagmeet Singh and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to keep the minority Liberal government afloat.
The plan is problematic on several fronts. It's not up to the federal government to tell provinces how to deliver health care. Its only obligation is to provide funding. By imposing their will on the provinces, the feds are once again challenging the underpinning of Confederation. They should stay in their lane.
Alberta Health Minister Adriana LaGrange told a news conference this week the province already has a better drug plan than the one Ottawa is proposing, including diabetes medications the feds plan to cover.
"I don't think the federal government understands that provinces do have processes in place. They're looking to provide something different, which adds bureaucracy and administrative burden on top of what we already have," she said.
In Ontario, Health Minister Sylvia Jones told the Legislature she hasn't seen details of the plan. "I've had no conversations, no feedback, no memos, nothing directly from the federal minister," she said. "We're waiting for that so I know exactly what we are looking at in terms of our responsibilities and the federal responsibilities."
Another troubling aspect to this is the cost. The parliamentary budget officer has estimated the incremental cost to federal and provincial governments combined to be $11.2 billion in 2024, increasing to $13.4 billion in 2027-28.
Russia and China are flexing their muscles.
There are wars in Gaza and Ukraine. Canada should be beefing up its military. Our NATO allies are expressing concern that, at 1.3% of the Gross Domestic Product, our contribution to the alliance lags woefully behind the 2% required of members. We're shortchanging our military at a time when we need it the most.
Pharmacare is a nice-to-have luxury.
Defence is a must-have essential.

What about ford?; Letters to the editor

Document 409 of 453

Rogan: Ditch Trudeau; 'I love Canadians, I just hate their government', controversial podcaster says

Author: Daniell, Mark

Publication info: Daniell, Mark. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/rogan-ditch-trudeau-i-love-canadians-just-hate/docview/2932311399/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Rogan%3A+Ditch+Trudeau%3B+%27I+love+Canadians%2C+I+just+hate+their+government%27%2C+controversial+podcaster+says&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-27&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Daniell%2C+Mark&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: A federal election isn't scheduled to happen until October 2025, but Joe Rogan is already warning Canadians: a vote for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau will quicken the country's continued slide.
Canada's household debt is now highest in the G7, with struggling Canadians creating more than 200,000 fundraising campaigns to cover everyday expenses.
The cost of food was up last fall despite the Industry Minister's claim that prices would "stabilize" for Canadians. In Toronto, prospective home buyers with a median annual income of $91,858 will have to save for more than 25 years for a down payment to buy a house.
Trudeau has become such a polarizing leader that last month, his security detail had to step in to usher him away after he was confronted by a group of protesters.
"If he gets elected again, you guys are gluttons for punishment," Rogan said during a chat with guest Tom Green on a recent episode of his Joe Rogan Experience podcast. "I love Canadians, I just hate their government. Pierre Poilievre ... that guy makes so much more sense. He's so common sense, calling out all the nonsense that's been done under this administration, which has been so sad to watch."
Green, who moved back to Ontario recently after living in Los Angeles for 20 years, gave Rogan a glimpse of what life is like inside Canada right now amid a growing political divide.
"This is so common in Canada ... driving around everywhere in the country, in the city, everywhere, people in pickup trucks (have) 'F---Trudeau'flags ... It says 'F---Trudeau'... Canada flag on it. People are mad ... he might not get elected the next time," Green said.
"I hope he doesn't," Rogan replied. But Green said the division in Canada isn't unlike what is happening in the United States as presumptive Republican presidential nominee Donald Trump gets ready for a rematch against incumbent Joe Biden this November.
"You've got Biden as the president now ... it's the same thing, half the country hates the party in power and they want to get him out. As a proud Canadian, I wanted to (make) the distinction that Canada's like (America). Everybody's arguing about issues, important issues, it's being reinforced through these algorithms. People get mad about it ... I sometimes (think), wouldn't it be interesting if Pierre Poilievre won the next election because all of a sudden we'd have a conservative government up there. Then, let's say Biden wins down here, you have a conservative government up there, and Tucker Carlson will be going up to Canada all of a sudden talking about how great we are. It can all turn on a dime."
Rogan and Green's exchange racked up thousands of views on social media and generated plenty of comments on Reddit, with some criticizing the podcaster for his sudden embrace of Poilievre.
"I guarantee Joe hasn't seen more than 10 mins of Pierre Poilievre speaking. He probably saw a few clips of him dunking on Trudeau and started swooning," one person wrote. "Trudeau has lost the country's approval, and Poilievre made a career out of speaking for Trudeau's haters. Nothing about Poilievre's personal campaign is why Joe likes him, it's entirely because he stands as an anti-Trudeau figure."
"Why would Joe care about the PM of Canada other than wanting right wing figures in charge everywhere? ... He has an agenda now," a second added.
Rogan has been critical of Trudeau for years, largely stemming from the prime minister's handling of the trucker protest in the nation's capital in 2022.
During a special broadcast of the Joe Rogan Experience podcast that aired during UFC 297 in Toronto last month, Rogan reacted to footage of the crowd inside Scotiabank Arena chanting "F---Trudeau!" "They're yelling 'F---Trudeau'," Rogan exclaimed.
The anti-Trudeau chants came after former UFC middleweight champion Sean Strickland sparred with a reporter during a pre-fight press conference and accused them of voting for the Liberals.
"Canada, get your s---together! Come back. Come back to what you used to be," Rogan urged.
The popular podcaster then made his endorsement for Canada's next prime minister. "That Pierre Poilievre guy, however you say his name, he makes sense," Rogan said.
On an episode of the Joe Rogan Experience in December with YouTuber Derek from More Plates More Dates, Rogan said he wouldn't cross the border until there's "an overhaul of government."
"I don't go up there anymore," Rogan said. "What they've done up there, what they did with the trucker rally and what Trudeau is doing with guns and what they're trying to clamp down on censorship on the Internet.
That guy can eat s---. That place needs 100% an overhaul of government. They're sliding down that dangerous road of communism. It scares the s---out of me."
Last summer, Rogan revealed at one point he might have moved north of the border, but said Trudeau's leadership scared him off.
"Canada's f---ing falling apart. All the s---they did during COVID was just the total wrong direction," he said on an episode of his podcast featuring Post Malone.
Recent polling shows a Liberal government under Trudeau heading for a massive defeat. According to the Sun's Warren Kinsella, "a couple of models conjured up in the past week show the Conservatives are on the cusp of the largest majority in Canadian history."
But whether it is ego, blind optimism or the fanciful notion that he can win next year, Trudeau has yet to indicate he'll step aside to try and save the Liberal Party from a defeat of truly historic proportions. mdaniell@postmedia.com

Location: Canada

Document 410 of 453

PM's Alberta trip full of imaginery enemies

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/pms-alberta-trip-full-imaginery-enemies/docview/2931444559/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=PM%27s+Alberta+trip+full+of+imaginery+enemies&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trudeau's trip to Alberta this week to announce $175 million in federal grants for housing starts probably did for federal credibility here (already at a low) what his 2018 Mr. Dressup Goes to Bollywood tour did for Canada-India relations.
Trudeau came without informing the provincial government. That's not exactly a legal necessity. This is, after all, his country, too. However, it is considered a courtesy for federal ministers to inform their provincial counterparts in advance.
Not telling the UCP government was deliberate.
Also, no provincial minister or MLA was invited to the housing announcement. Trudeau's lone Edmonton Liberal MP, Randy Boissonnault, was there. So too was his old friend, Mayor Amarjeet Sohi, who served in Trudeau's cabinet during its first term.
But, despite the fact that both the federal and provincial governments have largely put aside their differences to co-operate on housing affordability, there was no provincial representative in sight.
And while the P.M. had no time for a get-together with Alberta Premier Dainelle Smith, he and his staff did manage to find time for a 30-minute sit-down with "progressive" Edmonton podcaster, Ryan Jespersen.
It was the Jespersen interview that created the greatest anger among Albertans. Trudeau was at one and the same time arrogant, delusional, dismissive and insulting.
Time and again his answers betrayed his inner belief that his government's current unpopularity isn't its own fault. Poor polling has little to do with Liberal policies - carbon tax, EV mandate, inflation, housing and immigration - and everything to do with how others, notably the opposition Conservatives and online journalists, are being mean to them.
In Trudeau's mind, everything is a "comms" (communications) problem.
You can witness that thinking in last summer's cabinet shuffle, which changed no Liberal policies only the mouthpieces attempting to communicate them to voters.
And you can see it with the recent rebranding of the Climate Action Initiative Payment to the Canada Carbon Rebate. Surely the only reason 100% of Canadians aren't behind the Liberals'noble scheme is that they were too stupid to recognize what the old name was when they saw it on their bank statements.
Then Trudeau turned to insulting the intelligence of Albertans specifically.
In a patronizing tone that lasted throughout his Jespersen conversation, Trudeau said Albertans were being fooled by their own government and by online sharpies into disagreeing with his efforts to decarbonise Canada's economy.
He insisted we were being duped into not seeing how he was the true defender of our best interests, rather than the oil companies or Danielle Smith's government. For their own electoral and commercial self-interests, Trudeau claimed, these evil forces were conning people "to vote in ways that are not necessarily in their best interest."
Yet listeners had to wonder who was playing the fool? Trudeau insisted oil was rapidly becoming an unprofitable commodity, so Albertans'best chance for a prosperous future was to get behind such grandiose ideas as his net-zero power grid and his belief that pipelines are things of the past.
However, the International Energy Agency expects world oil demand to grow by a million barrels a day this year and to continue to grow until at least 2040, perhaps 2050.
In the working lifetimes of most Albertans, alternate energies will not provide the employment benefits of good old oil and natural gas. So who truly has Albertans'best interests at heart? Even while insisting he wasn't one of the "eastern bastards," Trudeau told listeners that Albertans were being manipulated by "conspiracy theorists" and pedlars of misinformation.
That's shorthand for "Hey, you hicks. I'm smarter than you." You need me and my government to guide you and regulate what you see online to keep you from harming yourselves, the planet and democracy.
Usually it's the people who see invisible plots and imaginary enemies, as Trudeau does, who need the help. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Document 411 of 453

Reading, writing and racial grievances; Divisive politics must be pushed out of the education system for good

Author: Li lley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/reading-writing-racial-grievances-divisive/docview/2931050909/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Reading%2C+writing+and+racial+grievances%3B+Divisive+politics+must+be+pushed+out+of+the+education+system+for+good&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.23&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When it comes to education in Ontario, the inmates are truly running the asylum. While the Doug Ford government has spent the last several years pushing an approach that goes back to the basics and focuses on student achievement, the people running the system have other ideas.
Now boards are even pushing back against orders from the Ministry of Education to cease and desist from using divisive, radical race-based political documents.
The latest example is the document put out by the Toronto District School Board titled "Facilitating Critical Conversations, a Teaching Resource for Challenging Oppression in Toronto District School Board Classrooms."
This is the document that claimed education is "a colonial structure that centres whiteness and Eurocentricity and therefore it must be actively decolonized." It also said that education favours white males and heterosexuals and "white supremacy is a structural reality that impacts all students and must be discussed and dismantled in classrooms, schools and communities."
Nothing like dividing people along race lines to try to end racism and bring everyone together.
On Wednesday, the office of Education Minister Stephen Lecce told the TDSB that this document is divisive and unacceptable, and the board promised to withdraw the document. Yet by late Wednesday, the TDSB put out a statement saying that the document would be "temporarily removed" and edited for future use.
The entire document is problematic and the ministry has said so, but the board is determined to move forward with this radical ideology.
This isn't new. We've seen it before in previous attempts to politicize education with radical ideology, such as the 2021 changes to the math curriculum that the Ford government had to reverse.
"Mathematics has been used to normalize racism and marginalization of non-Eurocentric mathematical knowledges and a decolonial, anti-racist approach to mathematics education makes visible its historical roots and social constructions," the revised curriculum said.
"In math, let's stick with math," the premier said at the time, while saying that portion of the curriculum would be removed.
Ford's view is the same as most parents, most teachers even, but the problem is that radical activists have taken up key leadership positions in the education system, from the teacher unions to the consultant class within school boards. These are the people who push this radical racebased agenda in every aspect of the school system.
Last year, the math lead for high school teachers at the TDSB, Jason To, led a webinar that put forward the idea that saying "2 + 2 = 4" was a covert example of white supremacy. This wasn't just a Toronto issue since To was backed up by teachers from Ottawa, Manitoulin Island and elsewhere across the province.
It's not just on race. It's on gender, class, politics and any number of other issues in which far-left politics are pushed into the classroom by activist teachers, activist curriculum consultants and activist principals.
Lecce has described efforts to deal with these outbursts as akin to playing whack-a-mole - you strike one down and a new radical or collection of radicals rise up.
We can talk about racism in our classrooms, and we can discuss gender issues, class issues and even politics, but it should not be the indoctrination of students by one radical ideology, which unfortunately is what is happening time and again.
If parents are losing faith in the education system, this is part of the reason why. Parents send their children to school for an education and not an indoctrination, but the activists think otherwise.
It is parents, not the activist edu-crats, who Lecce and the rest of the Ford government should listen to. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 412 of 453

Choking on carbon tax; It's a clear loser when other Liberals won't defend it

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/choking-on-carbon-tax-clear-loser-when-other/docview/2931050621/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Choking+on+carbon+tax%3B+It%27s+a+clear+loser+when+other+Liberals+won%27t+defend+it&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It appears that Justin Trudeau's carbon tax is so unpopular that other Liberals won't even say if they support it.
Meanwhile, Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre continues to ride high in the polls with his promise to axe the tax.
It wasn't that long ago that in certain political circles a carbon tax was seen as a no-brainer.
Now it's just a loser. Bonnie Crombie, elected as leader of the Ontario Liberals at the beginning of December, denied support for Trudeau's tax several times in what can only be seen as a disastrous first week at Queen's Park.
Crombie was asked about her position on a carbon tax at least seven times during a news conference at Queen's Park and couldn't give an answer or opinion beyond saying she'd consult. Then she went on CTV's Power Play and refused to answer questions about her position on a carbon tax at least nine times.
While at Queen's Park, she was asked about a provincial tax. On CTV, Crombie wouldn't even answer questions about the federal carbon tax.
Imagine that: The leader of the Liberal party in Canada's biggest province can't even endorse a signature policy of the federal Liberal party - the carbon tax.
There was a time when we were told you couldn't win in Canadian politics unless you had a plan to put a price on carbon with a tax, capand-trade system or some other scheme. Of course, this was never true, it was just the view of the urban Laurentian Elite who populate much of the media and chattering classes.
Doug Ford handily won Ontario's election in 2018 promising to rip up the Liberal cap-andtrade system. Jason Kenney swept to power in Alberta promising to eliminate the NDP carbon tax in that province.
There also has never been a carbon tax provincially in Saskatchewan, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Now, even at the federal level, the carbon tax that Trudeau still boasts of is far from popular and Poilievre is holding onto a substantial lead in the polls with his promise to axe the tax. Polling even shows that more people think the tax hurts them financially compared to those who think it benefits them - which is the government's ongoing false message.
An Abacus Data poll of 2,199 adults taken in January showed that 43% of Canadians thought they'd save money if the carbon tax is eliminated, 20% thought they'd lose money and 37% didn't know. A further 47% said they believed that the carbon tax was a "big reason why prices for many things has risen so much." Just 26% disagreed with the idea that the carbon tax was responsible for increasing prices.
In short, the carbon tax, Trudeau's signature policy, has become a political loser.
Close to 20 years ago, a friend of mine in the polling industry said that Canadians were kind of odd on environmental issues. If the economy was going well, they'd say climate change and the environment were among the most important issues facing the country, but they were less interested in those issues as things got worse, as job losses mounted or prices rose.
That is what has happened here and the Trudeau Liberals - and their allies like Crombie in Ontario - haven't realized this and still try to cling to policies that will no longer get them votes.
In fact, holding onto the idea of a carbon tax in 2024 will only drag a political party lower. Let's hope that Trudeau and Crombie hug that carbon tax as tightly as they can. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 413 of 453

Political mismatch; Ford benefiting from opposition leaders' weak start to session

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/political-mismatch-ford-benefiting-opposition/docview/2929297895/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Political+mismatch%3B+Ford+benefiting+from+opposition+leaders%27+weak+start+to+session&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-21&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Successful politicians who win multiple elections often do so because they face weak opposition leaders. That was true for Doug Ford before Bonnie Crombie was elected to lead the Ontario Liberals and it looks like it's still true today.
Ford has been spending a lot of time talking about Crombie lately, including dubbing her the "Queen of the Carbon Tax." It's a name Crombie has rejected, saying she hasn't spoken out in favour of the carbon tax. But, on Tuesday, she fell flat when asked about her position.
"Can you clarify your position on the carbon tax?" reporter Jessica Smith Cross, from news website The Trillium, asked Crombie.
It's a perfectly reasonable question and given all of the attention Ford has been placing on Crombie and the carbon tax, she should have been ready to answer. In some ways, she was ready as she looked down and read her carefully scripted response from the page in front of her, but Crombie's response clarified nothing. She offered a rambling attack on Ford before promising consultations.
"We are going to consult with our residents, with stakeholders, with scientists and Ontario families and we will develop a strong and a progressive plan to urgently tackle the issue of climate change," Crombie said.
"It's hard to imagine an adult these days having not formed an opinion at this point in their lives on whether carbon taxes are a good thing or not. Why are you not sharing your opinion with us, or do you actually not have one?" Smith Cross responded.
Crombie again said she'd consult and come back with a "strong and progressive plan" for fighting climate change. Pushed several more times, Crombie wouldn't say yes or no on whether she supports a carbon tax or opposes one.
This should have been simple for Crombie. She shouldn't have had to read from the page. She shouldn't have sounded unsure. She could have simply said that since Ford ripped up Ontario's cap-and-trade program that the federal carbon tax has been put in place and there is no need for a provincial one.
Instead, she walked into the trap Ford had set for her with his gimmicky legislation to require a referendum on a future carbon tax. It was a very weak first day for Crombie, who hasn't moved her party in the polls despite improving Liberal fundraising somewhat.
As for the Official Opposition, Marit Stiles of the NDP showed once again why her party isn't ever getting near power.
Stiles opened the first question period in more than two months asking the Ford government about Bill 124, the wage bill recently struck down by the courts. This is an issue that is only a vote-mover for a segment of the public service that is already voting for the NDP.
The only way the NDP can move from the opposition benches to the government side is by expanding its reach beyond unionized government workers. Yet, given the chance to ask about any other issue, it once again focused on the same issue it has been harping on for five years.
Stiles could have asked about the crisis in primary care and the shortage of family doctors, the problems in the daycare system, any number of issues affecting the lives of average Ontario residents, but she didn't.
Most days, the NDP at Queen's Park sounds like it's a lobby group for government workers and that's it.
Ford's government isn't running on all cylinders at this point. It has had missteps and many of the problems it has promised to fix, like housing affordability and supply, haven't been resolved.
What Ford has going for him is that his opponents are weak, and they both showed it on Monday. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Document 414 of 453

Roll Back The Clock; Protesters return to Parliament Hill 2 years after convoy rally

Author: Glass, Marlo

Publication info: Glass, Marlo. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/roll-back-clock-protesters-return-parliament-hill/docview/2928069818/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Roll+Back+The+Clock%3B+Protesters+return+to+Parliament+Hill+2+years+after+convoy+rally&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Glass%2C+Marlo&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Hundreds of protesters converged on Parliament Hill on Saturday, two years after thousands gridlocked downtown Ottawa.
Streets were filled with signs and flags reading "Welcome back, truckers," "Trudeau must go," and "Protect the children," among others.
While the original convoy protest brought wall-to-wall big-rig trucks and pickups to the nation's capital only a few of them were driven down Wellington Street on Saturday, one of them honking a train-like horn.
Demonstrators were, for the most part, well-behaved, gathering on Parliament Hill's front lawn under bright sunshine for speeches and tunes from a DJ. A brief dance party broke out. Outbursts of "Freedom!" chants peppered the gathering, and a firework was shot off.
Some protesters were turned away from entering Parliament Hill as they were carrying massive flags taped to hockey sticks or other long staffs. Rules for demonstrations on the Hill require all signs and banners to be hand-held on poles no more than two metres in length. "Obscene" signs may be banned, as well as signs depicting blood or graphic violence.
Several "F---Trudeau" flags were flown, as well as inverted Canadian flags, commonly known as a sign of distress. Protesters also carried signs against government-mandated ID programs and teaching sexual orientation and gender identity in schools. Some flags and signs promoted conspiracy theories that took root during the COVID-19 pandemic, ranging from anti-vaccine theories to railing against the World Economic Forum and "The Great Reset."
According to the Anti-Defamation League, an advocacy group specializing in civil rights law and combatting antisemitism and extremism, "The Great Reset" warns of "global elites" pushing an agenda of globalism to destroy national sovereignty.
The theory says the COVID-19 pandemic was used as a tool to usher in lockdown measures, restrict freedom, impose mass surveillance and hurt small businesses.
"One can see long-standing and familiar right-wing conspiracy tropes at play in the Great Reset conspiracy, including the notion that a group of elites are working to undermine national sovereignty and individual freedoms, references to a "New World Order," and the idea that these malicious actors will seek to exploit a catastrophic incident - like a global pandemic - to advance their agenda," the ADL wrote in late 2020. "A common undercurrent among proponents is a mistrust of vaccines, which they believe will be forced upon people to fulfill the nefarious objectives of Bill Gates or the pharmaceutical industry."
Flags with former U.S. president Donald Trump's "Make America Great Again" slogan were also flown on Parliament Hill on Saturday, along with a smattering of U.S. flags.
The convoy protesters left the Hill at about 2 p.m. to march through the downtown district and back, with a smaller faction of the group continuing through the ByWard Market.
At approximately the same time, a different group of pro-Palestinian protesters gathered at the Human Rights Monument near Ottawa City Hall before marching to Israel's embassy on O'Connor Street. The group was protesting what participants described as the Israel Defence Force's "horrific attacks" on Rafah, once designated as one of the last remaining safe zones in Gaza following the Oct. 7 Hamas attacks in Israel and subsequent war.
The two protesting groups were directed away from each other by Ottawa police. The city's bylaw department had warned earlier in the day that safety would be "paramount" during protests and enforcement would focus on megaphones, blocking of roadways and use of fireworks.
As they marched, convoy protesters used megaphones and blared sirens, many of them jeering Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.
"The racist regime of Trudeau must go," the crowd shouted as it proceeded along Rideau Street.
Rebecca Sheppard of Stand4Thee, an organization that has accused Trudeau of treason, was among dozens of speakers to addressed the crowd on Parliament Hill Sheppard asked the crowd to "cover the little ones'ears" before she launched into a string of expletives against Trudeau and "every one of those Liberal executive branch members who illegally invoked the Emergencies Act in 2022 and attempted to overthrow the government by doing so, infringed on our rights, broke the law, hurt people, stole property and acted as if they were God themselves."
She said the government needed to be help accountable, and her rights "come from God, not the government."
The 2022 protests, which featured big-rig trucks in the downtown core, were spearheaded by some who opposed the COVID-19 pandemic restrictions, many of which had ended by that time. Others hoped to bring down the Trudeau government.
Police from across Ontario and Quebec were brought in to clear the Ottawa streets in a massive multi-day operation that ended Feb. 17, 2022.
Invoking the Emergencies Act triggered an automatic federal commission to review the government's decision. The Public Order Emergency Commission issued 56 recommendations for police and governments last February, and commissioner Paul Rouleau concluded that the Liberals were justified in turning to the never-before-used legislation.
- With files from The Canadian Press and The Associated Press

Document 415 of 453

It won't make a difference; Painful truth about Trudeau's 'rebranded'carbon tax

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wont-make-difference-painful-truth-about-trudeaus/docview/2928069031/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=It+won%27t+make+a+difference%3B+Painful+truth+about+Trudeau%27s+%27rebranded%27carbon+tax&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-18&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: It's absurd that Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and the Liberals believe that rebranding their "climate action incentive payments" as "Canada carbon rebates" is what's needed to fix their carbon tax.
What Canadians deserve is the truth about Trudeau's carbon tax, which they've never been given by the Liberals.
Consider: The Liberals say if Conservative leader Pierre Poilievre axes the carbon tax, people will lose the rebates.
In reality, if there's no carbon tax, there's no need for rebates.
The Liberals describe their carbon tax as a "price on pollution."
It's not a price on pollution.
It's a price on almost all goods and services Canadians pay for because almost all goods and services are produced using fossil fuel energy, including necessities, such as heating homes in winter.
The Liberals claim 80% of households in the eight provinces paying the carbon tax (Quebec and British Columbia have separate systems) receive more in rebates than they pay in carbon taxes.
In fact, Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux says that when the negative impact of the carbon tax on the economy is factored in, 60% of households currently pay more in carbon taxes than they receive in rebates, which will rise to 80% in Nova Scotia in 2025, 80% in Ontario in 2026, 80% in Manitoba in 2029 and 80% in Alberta and Prince Edward Island in 2030.
Here are the PBO's estimated average net costs (after rebates) for households in provinces under the federal carbon tax regime this year, with the carbon tax at $65 per tonne of emissions, followed by the estimated net cost in 2030, when it will be $170 per tonne: Alberta $710, $2,773; Ontario: $478, $1,820; Saskatchewan $410, $1723; Manitoba $386, $1490; Nova Scotia $431, $1,513; P.E.I $465, $1,521; and Newfoundland and Labrador $347, $1,316.
The PBO's calculations didn't include New Brunswick because it joined the federal carbon tax system after it did these estimates.
The federal carve-out on home heating oil will reduce the costs in Atlantic Canada to some extent, but all households will pay more when the Trudeau government hikes the carbon tax by 23% to $80 per tonne of emissions, from the current $65 per tonne, on April 1.
The Trudeau government argues that the PBO failed to factor in the economic costs of not addressing climate change.
Little impact
In fact, the PBO says, "Canada's own emissions (at 1.5% of the global total) are not large enough to materially impact climate change" and, "consequently, Canada's primary means of limiting the economic costs of climate change are through participation in a globally coordinated emissions reduction regime."
That means we're relying on countries like China - the world's largest emitter, which burns more coal than the rest of the world combined - to lower their emissions.
The idea that the increased costs faced by Canadians paying Trudeau's carbon tax up to 2030 - we don't know what happens after that - will result in savings because of less severe weather in Canada by 2030, when our emissions aren't large enough to materially impact climate change, is absurd.
The Trudeau government says its carbon tax is revenue neutral, with 90% of the money raised returned to households and 10% going to small businesses, Indigenous groups, farmers, schools, universities and municipalities.
But the carbon tax isn't revenue neutral because the Trudeau government doesn't rebate the GST households pay on top of the carbon tax.
The PBO estimates this "tax on a tax" will raise $486 million for the federal government this year, rising annually to more than $1 billion in 2030.
The Canadian Federation of Independent Business says the Trudeau government hasn't returned $2.5 billion in carbon tax revenues collected since 2019 to small businesses in Ontario, Manitoba, Saskatchewan and Alberta, despite promises to do so.
Indigenous leaders in Ontario have asked the federal court for a judicial review of the carbon tax, arguing it discriminates against First Nations by disproportionately impacting them, without appropriate financial relief.
Farmers complain that the financial relief the government has provided to defray the added costs they face because of the impact of the carbon tax on agriculture, is inadequate.
The Liberals say their carbon tax is the most effective way to lower industrial greenhouse gas emissions linked to climate change.
In reality, we don't know how effective the carbon tax is because the Trudeau government doesn't keep track of its impact on emissions.
It guesstimates it will account for up to one-third of emission reduction in 2030.
According to the latest available federal government data, Canada's emissions in 2021 went up by 1.8% to 670 million tonnes, compared to 2020.
The United States, without a national carbon tax, has been more successful than Canada at reducing emissions. lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Small business; Emissions; Carbon; Decades; Judicial reviews; Environmental tax; Rebates; Cost control; Households; Climate change

Business indexing term: Subject: Small business Environmental tax Rebates Cost control

Anti-Semitism downplayed; CP, fed appointee defend protesters

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/anti-semitism-downplayed-cp-fed-appointee-defend/docview/2927931867/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Anti-Semitism+downplayed%3B+CP%2C+fed+appointee+defend+protesters&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The people who targeted a Jewish hospital in Toronto this week with calls for the death of Jews seem awfully concerned about how people see them.
They've been pushing back against those denouncing them with key help from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's appointed representative on Islamophobia and a national media organization.
Amira Elghawaby, appointed just over a year ago as Canada's first special representative on combatting Islamophobia, posted to social media earlier this week that those calling out the Monday protests at Toronto's Mount Sinai Hospital were going too far.
Elghawaby said that "hospitals and medical workers must always be safe to provide important services," but then went on to defend the protests.
"Yet, also troubling and wrong is the rush to label protesters as anti-Semitic and/or terrorist sympathizers," Elghawaby said.
No, not really, because that is what these protesters have shown themselves to be since Hamas launched the war on Oct. 7.
Within hours of the brutal terrorist attacks taking place, one of the organizing groups of Monday's protests, the Palestinian Youth Movement, was organizing a rally to celebrate the attacks.
"We call upon our people in the far diaspora in Toronto to uplift our resistance and our martyrs," the group posted across social media.
A post to Instagram from Oct.
7, still visible as of this writing, celebrates the taking of "over 30 Zionist hostages" and "the fall of settlements surrounding Gaza" and "our right to resist by any means necessary."
It then asks people to rally at Nathan Phillips Square on Oct. 9 in support of these acts.
Thousands did and thousands more have since the attack.
I've attended many of these rallies and while they'll call on Israel to agree to a ceasefire, I've never heard anyone at them call for the release of hostages, for Hamas to surrender or for them to lay down arms.
There are no denunciations of Hamas'terror attacks - instead it's all about cheering on the intefadeh, the resistance, or the martyrs, which means cheering on the killing of Jews or those who kill the Jews.
Now, this group wants us to believe when their march goes along hospital row in Toronto that they were just "walking by a hospital" as the many emails flooding the inboxes of so many politicians at all levels are hearing? After leaders, including Trudeau, Premier Doug Ford and Toronto Mayor Olivia Chow, denounced the actions, the groups got defensive.
"Organizers of a pro-Palestinian demonstration that passed by a Toronto hospital earlier this week are accusing the prime minister and the city's mayor of spreading misinformation and being unfair to peaceful protesters," read a story from The Canadian Press (CP), wire service published Wednesday.
Embarrassing It's a story that is utterly embarrassing for CP and should have their editors asking tough questions.
I can tell those editors that tough questions are being asked about CP and the diminishing value of its work in media companies.
How do you describe this as a march that "passed by a Toronto hospital" without adding context, or telling the truth? Mount Sinai is in an area called hospital row on University Ave.
in the middle of the city. Only the Jewish hospital was targeted, not SickKids, not Princess Margaret, not Toronto General, and yet we're supposed to take this as people simply passing by? They also didn't just pass by - protesters climbed on top of the awning to the hospital entrance, someone hoisted a Palestinian flag to the top and waved it as the mob of yobs shouted "Long live the intefadeh," while at least one of their cohort banged a military sounding beat on a snare drum.
Does that sound like walking by to you or does that sound like the purposeful targeting of not only a hospital but a Jewish hospital? It's disgusting this group would attempt to shift blame for their actions and their anti-Semitism, it's worse still that a government appointee and a national and supposedly unbiased media outlet would try to help them in their cause. blilley@postmedia.com

On the road with Steven Guilbeault

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/on-road-with-steven-guilbeault/docview/2927756460/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=On+the+road+with+Steven+Guilbeault&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: To understand why many Canadians have trouble trusting the Trudeau government, look no further than Environment and Climate Change Minister Steven Guilbeault's evolving position on whether the federal government intends to continue funding roads.
On Monday, as reported by the Montreal Gazette, Guilbeault said in a speech in Montreal to a public transit conference: "Our government has made the decision to stop investing at the federal level in new road infrastructure.
"Of course, we will continue to be there for cities, provinces and territories to maintain the existing network, but there will be no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network. The analysis we have done is that the network is perfectly adequate to respond to the needs we have ... We can very well achieve our goals of economic, social and human development without more enlargement of the road network."
Responding to the political controversy this caused since this would be a major shift in federal policy that caught other levels of government by surprise - see the consternation expressed by Ontario Premier Doug Ford and Alberta Premier Danielle Smith - Guilbeault claimed Wednesday he was only talking about large, mega-projects.
"I should have been more specific," Guilbeault said. "But in that same conference, I specified that we still have funds, obviously, to maintain and enhance our road network across the country ... I was talking specifically about projects like the Troisième Lien that the CAQ government in Quebec wants to move forward with, which is something myself and many of my Quebec cabinet colleagues have said time and time again. This is not a new policy at all."
That's nice, but that's not what he said on Monday. His two statements directly contradict one another.
It recalls the political firestorm Prime Minister Justin Trudeau caused in 2017 when he said his climate change policies would eventually "phase out" Alberta's oilsands, which went down about as well as you'd expect in Alberta.
Trudeau later said he "misspoke" and "said something the way I shouldn't have said it."
The Liberals remind us of Humpty Dumpty in Lewis Carroll's Through the Looking-Glass, who scornfully explained: "When I use a word, it means just what I choose it to mean - neither more nor less."

Injection of racism; In Canada, anti-drug site protester told to go back where he came from

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/injection-racism-canada-anti-drug-site-protester/docview/2926959519/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Injection+of+racism%3B+In+Canada%2C+anti-drug+site+protester+told+to+go+back+where+he+came+from&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.13&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Nothing defines the mess Canada is in with its zombie drug crisis more than an activist telling a father of Chinese descent who's opposed to a drug injection site to go back to where he came from.
So far, no tweets from Prime Minister Justin Trudeau calling out the racist behaviour.
In fact, there has been no media coverage at all.
Thanks to Opposition Leader Pierre Poilievre going to social media, perhaps that will change. And, thanks to social media, it's going to be hard to sweep this video under the rug - let alone keep it from going viral.
"You're what's wrong with Canada," a woman said to a man outside a meeting about installing one of these safe-consumption locations in his Richmond, B.C., neighbourhood. "Go back to where you came from."
The man is Canadian, and he lives in British Columbia, and he has every right to question the notion of helping drug addicts stay addicted and die.
But this woman doesn't think so. "This is not what Canada does," she said. "Canada fights for our citizens ... Go back to Hong Kong."
The crowd responded by yelling: "No drugs, no drugs." The RCMP stood there helpless as things got worse.
Trudeau, who has many tweets in support of the Asian community during this week's Lunar New Year celebrations, really should call this out the same way he called out the swastika found during the Freedom Convoy or when Conservatives were seen walking with truckers in protest.
There should be no double standard on racism.
Not only was this man discriminated against because of his ethnicity, the woman went further by saying to the largely Asian demonstration: "All of you are corrupt. The drugs come from you."
One look at the streets of Vancouver and it's clear the drugs aren't coming from the people in this crowd opposed to the drugs but from those who foster the use of them by handing out free supply and drug kits, including needles, that help people do drugs.
They create crime, hardship and despair.
We see this here in the Greater Toronto Area too. Drugs are ruining society and the approach currently employed is failing miserably.
Poilievre knows this and is vowing to change it.
"Radical activist spews racist tirade against a Chinese man trying to protect kids from a drug injection site the NDP & Liberal governments are forcing into Richmond. Help me fight against the NDP/Liberals pushing drugs on the Chinese community," he posted on his X account.
He also linked to a motion to "stop the drug injection site in Richmond" that states that "after eight years of Justin Trudeau's taxpayer-funded distribution of hard drugs has caused harm and addiction" and the "sites are grounds for criminal activity and make neighbourhoods unsafe," Conservatives call on the government to "stop handing our taxpayer funds to addicts."
But history has shown the people who believe in this "harm reduction strategy" won't do that.
On treating a fellow Canadian of Asian descent so horribly, history shows the racism charges only come to people like the Convoy truckers who are opposed to Liberal policies.
People pushing progressive policies like free drugs for addicts seem to skate.
