Document 419 of 453

Clock's ticking down on Trudeau

Author: Goldstein, Lorrie

Publication info: Goldstein, Lorrie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/clocks-ticking-down-on-trudeau/docview/2926957922/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Clock%27s+ticking+down+on+Trudeau&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=Goldstein%2C+Lorrie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Time is rapidly running out for Prime Minister Justin Trudeau to decide whether he's going to lead the Liberals into the next federal election.
Assuming the Liberal/NDP accord holds, it has to be held on or before Oct. 20, 2025 - 20 months away.
But Trudeau has to make a decision about his own future well before that.
If he's not going to run, he should be announcing it for the good of his party right about now.
Speculation about Trudeau's future to date has hinged on whether his confidence-and-supply agreement with NDP leader Jagmeet Singh until the fall of 2025 will hold, given Singh's frequent threats to break it - the latest on the issue of delivering pharmacare.
But the end of the accord wouldn't automatically mean a snap election.
In addition, does the NDP really want to provoke an election now, which if the polls are accurate, would result in a huge Conservative majority where the NDP would have no say? The real deadline facing Trudeau is that the longer he waits to decide on his future, the more likely that decision will be made for him by default.
That is, he will have to stay on as leader because there won't be enough time to hold a Liberal leadership race to replace him, or for a new leader to put his or her stamp on the party and convince Canadians to vote for it.
Twenty years ago, then-Progressive Conservative prime minister Brian Mulroney waited until February 1993 to announce he was resigning as party leader, prior to a federal election that had to be called by October 1993 - eight months later.
The party didn't elect its new leader - Kim Campbell - until June 1993.
In the ensuing federal election four months later on Oct. 25, 1993, the PCs were reduced from a majority government to two seats.
That marked the beginning of the end for the PC party as a viable force in federal politics and the start of a Liberal dynasty that lasted 13 years.
The eight months between Mulroney resigning and Campbell leading the party into its disastrous showing in the 1993 election wasn't enough time for the PCs to put angry voter memories of the Mulroney years behind them, or for Campbell to create her own political brand and run an effective election campaign.
Given months of recent polling indicating Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives would win a huge majority government if an election was held today, the next Liberal leader, if there is one, is going to need a lot more than eight months to campaign for the Liberal leadership, put the memory of the Trudeau years behind him, or her, and convince voters a Liberal government under their leadership would be different from the current one.
Leadership races suck up money and resources that would otherwise be used in an election, as well as creating internal divisions within the party the new leader has to be able to mend, which takes time.
Given these realities, if Trudeau is going to resign before the next election, he should be announcing it now, to give his party any realistic chance of winning.
Releasing a poll last week showing the Conservatives with a 19-point lead over the Liberals, Abacus Data CEO David Coletto said the Liberals'root problem is that "most people aren't listening anymore to Trudeau."
The question is, will they listen to any new Liberal leader? lgoldstein@postmedia.com @sunlorrie

Subject: Leadership; Prime ministers

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Identifier / keyword: rapidly; running; prime; minister; justin; trudeau

Title: Clock's ticking down on Trudeau

Publication title: The Ottawa Sun; Ottawa

First page: A.17

Document 420 of 453

End of the road; Enviro minister says feds done forking over cash for highways

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/end-road-enviro-minister-says-feds-done-forking/docview/2926957810/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=End+of+the+road%3B+Enviro+minister+says+feds+done+forking+over+cash+for+highways&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.12&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Steven Guilbeault said the quiet part out loud - the Trudeau government doesn't want any more roads.
If you think traffic is bad now, just keep this guy in power for a few more years.
Guilbeault, of course, is Prime Minister Justin Trudeau's fanatical environment minister, a former Greenpeace activist famous for getting arrested, someone I first met more than 20 years ago when he locked himself to a gas pump in Montreal to demand we all stop driving cars.
Some things never change, Guilbeault is still pushing that agenda today "Our government has made the decision to stop investing in new road infrastructure," Guilbeault told a Montreal environmental conference. "Of course, we will continue to be there for cities, provinces and territories to maintain the existing network, but there will be no more envelopes from the federal government to enlarge the road network."
Instead of giving infrastructure money to provinces and municipalities to build new roads, something the federal government has done for decades, Guilbeault says they'll only invest in what he calls "active and public transit" going forward. Translation: more bike lanes, more walking, more buses, but no more money for roads.
That's something that caught the eye of Premier Doug Ford, who despite working well with the Trudeau government, said he was shocked by Guilbeault's comments.
"I'm gobsmacked," Ford posted on X with a link to the Guilbeault story. "A federal minister said they won't invest in new roads or highways. He doesn't care that you're stuck in bumper-to-bumper traffic. I do. We're building roads and highways, with or without a cent from the feds."
He's in the middle of trying to build Hwy. 413, a new link connecting Toronto's fast-growing western suburbs.
That's a project Guilbeault has expressed opposition to in the past and it's controversial with the Liberals and NDP, though popular with voters.
In the last provincial election, Ford and his PC Party won every single seat in the vicinity of the highway. This happened despite the provincial Liberals and NDP campaigning hard against the project and at least two years of almost uniformly negative media headlines where the highway was constantly described as "controversial."
Turns out, the highway isn't controversial and people like their cars, but that's something Guilbeault would know since he loves his car as well.
As we reported in 2020, Guilbeault was logging more than 3,000 km per month shortly after he was elected and appointed minister in the 2019 election. That's more driving in seven months than the 15,000 km the average Canadian drives in a year.
What makes that even more shocking is that Guilbeault had said before he was elected that he wanted to try to serve as a minister without taking the car and chauffeur.
"I never needed to have a car," Guilbeault told Quebec radio station QUB in 2019. "I don't want to make a big 'no, I'll never do that'statement, but I would try to do without. Why not? Maybe it won't work, but at least I'll try it."
A lot of those clicks on the car came from his driver shuttling him back and forth between Ottawa and Montreal despite there being a perfectly good train available.
"We can very well achieve our goals of economic, social and human development without more enlargement of the road network," he said Tuesday.
Maybe Guilbeault thinks that because Montreal, where he lives, is all built up. Other parts of the country are not, and some are growing, adding massive new populations, which will require expanding the road network.
You can't add a million people over the last couple of years as Ontario has done and not expand infrastructure, like roads. That is unless you want everyone stuck in traffic or crammed into a train while Guilbeault gets chauffeured past you. Hypocrite.

Subject: Automobiles; Infrastructure; Traffic; Decades; Roads & highways

'Absolutely terrible'; Pro-Hamas supporters surrounding Jewish hospital a hate crime

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/absolutely-terrible-pro-hamas-supporters/docview/2926317657/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Absolutely+terrible%27%3B+Pro-Hamas+supporters+surrounding+Jewish+hospital+a+hate+crime&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-14&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When you have a female Jewish doctor's car surrounded by Pro-Hamas protesters shouting "intefadeh," while blocking people at an iconic hospital with Jewish roots, it's no longer a protest but a hate crime.
The prime minister, premier, police and medical community agree.
"Interfering with the operations of a hospital is not acceptable," said Toronto Police, adding that officers are "investigating several incidents that occurred in front of Mount Sinai Hospital."
Urging "decency," Premier Doug Ford called out masked, Pro-Hamas protesters who pushed things too far Monday night in front of the "sacred" Jewish-inspired hospital. One demonstrator even scaled the front of Mount Sinai and got up on the University Ave. hospital's awning. Dr. David Jacobs posted on X about a female doctor of Jewish faith seeing her car "swarmed" with people "banging on it."
But with only two police officers present, they got away.
"I think it's absolutely terrible," said Premier Doug Ford. "Folks, get some decency. Have common sense. These hospitals are there to save lives. Mount Sinai did everything they could to save my brother's life - he was in there.
"They are an incredible hospital - along with all the hospitals across the province," he added. "They are there to help people ... do not break the law. Because that is exactly what happened, you broke the law."
Videos certainly support Ford's contention.
"As we have said before, officers use their discretion during large crowd demonstrations and even if arrests are not deemed safe to make at the time, investigations will continue and charges can be laid at a later date," said Toronto Police spokesperson Stephanie Sayer.
"TPS will be providing an increased police presence along hospital row to ensure that essential hospital services and emergency routes remain accessible" and they are "appealing to anyone who witnessed the events (Monday) evening or who experienced harassment to contact police."
There was also a counter-protest by an emergency room physician, Dr. Raghu Venugopal, who first posted on X that he was coming to the demonstration and later put up a video explaining such behaviour was inappropriate on such "hallowed" ground.
Richard Robertson, director of research and advocacy at B'nai Brith Canada noted that "the hospital was founded during a dark period in Canadian history when Jewish doctors were excluded from working at other hospitals."
He said this was "a new low for anti-Israel protesters in Canada," and added that the "sanctity of the patients, their loved ones, and those vested with the responsibility of tending to the sick, was violated." Mayor Olivia Chow, who has undergone cancer treatment at Mount Sinai, took to social media to say there's no place in Toronto for such hate.
In April 2005, Chow, then a Toronto councillor, told the CBC that she underwent surgery the previous year for thyroid cancer.
Surgeons removed her thyroid gland, and she later received radioactive iodine treatment to deal with any lingering cancer cells.
"My family has a long history with Mount Sinai. My father passed there, both my late husband (former federal NDP leader Jack Layton) and I had cancer treatments there," the municipal leader posted on X. "(Monday's) actions at Mount Sinai Hospital, a hospital founded by Toronto's Jewish community, were unacceptable. Targeting Jewish institutions is anti-Semitic and hate has no place in our city."
Councillor Brad Bradford called it "full-blown anti-Semitism" and urged Chow to "end this anti-Semitism intimidation."
Referencing a federal government law against protesting at hospitals, Ford implored people to stop.
"I don't care what you are protesting," said the premier. "You want to protest, go to City Hall, come down to Queen's Park, jump up and down, do whatever you want."
I asked a second question about the blatant anti-Semitism of this action, and what happened earlier in the evening when Aroma Cafe was vandalized once again.
"It doesn't matter if it's Islamophobia (or) anti-Semitism, folks, we have 120 nationalities here in Ontario, 200 languages are spoken," said Ford. "We pride ourselves around the world for everyone to come here and get along and live side by side, work side by side.
"We need to live peacefully together. That's my message to the people of Ontario: We live in a peaceful society. I am asking everyone, please get along. Let's work together. Get along."
Prime Minister Justin Trudeau agreed in an X post Tuesday.
"The demonstration at Mount Sinai Hospital yesterday was reprehensible. Hospitals are places for treatment and care, not protests and intimidation," said Trudeau. "I strongly condemn this display of anti-Semitism. In Toronto and across Canada, we stand with Jewish communities against this hate."
Many other leaders commented. "An embassy? Nope. A consulate? Nope. A government office? Nope," Conservative deputy leader Melissa Lantsman, the MP for Thornhill, posted on X. "The mob chose patients, their families, nurses, doctors and all those helping to save lives inside of a hospital. They chose Jews, because it's Mount Sinai Hospital. So gross."
The outrage crossed party lines. "Targeting Mount Sinai Hospital to incite intifada against Israel and the Jewish people is flagrantly anti-Semitic and undermines the health and safety of the patients and medical professionals," Liberal MP Marco Mendicino, who respresents Eglinton-Lawrence, posted on X. "The torrent of hatred against Jews and Jewish institutions must stop!" Deborah Lyons, Canada's special envoy on stamping out anti-Semitism and Holocaust denialism, wrote on X that she planned to call "Toronto's mayor and chief of police today to discuss how they will put a stop to this despicable targeting and attempted intimidation of Mount Sinai Hospital and Jews across Toronto and Canada."
Independent MP Kevin Vuong demanded to know "what action, steps" Chow was gong to take to protect health-care workers, patients and members of the Jewish community.
"Has she done the absolute bare minimum of ensuring @TorontoPolice's clear on their responsibility to protect hospitals & health-care workers per Bill C3?" he posted on X.
Based on what has happened since Hamas' Oct. 7 slaughter of innocent Israeli's, when it comes to anti-Semitism in Toronto, it will likely happen again. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Whining conservatives need to grow up

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/whining-conservatives-need-grow-up/docview/2925767938/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Whining+conservatives+need+to+grow+up&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If you want to criticize Justin Trudeau, you'll have to get behind me in line.
But some of you conservatives need to grow up.
CTV News reports, "An Ontario brewery that hosted Prime Minister Justin Trudeau says it's been inundated with hundreds of hateful messages since then."
Trudeau visited the Brewer's Pantry in Bowmanville, Ont., to show support for Robert Rock, the Liberal Party candidate for the Durham federal by-election.
Brewer's Pantry posted on social media Friday: "I am not political. I am not a politician. I do not have thick skin. These are comments about my business that I am passionate about, and very proud of. Your comments and abusive phone calls hurt me."
One user wrote on Facebook: "Small business owners in Bowmanville will not forget your stance. You are not one of us."
To that person, I would say you don't represent the entire business community. Don't think so highly of yourself that you assume your hateful nature is that of everyone else.
Some of your fellow businesspeople are Liberals. Get over it.
You don't have to like Trudeau - I certainly don't - but if you are actually a business person I am surprised to learn you can survive if you will only do business with those with whom you agree.
And a note especially to those people who post such messages anonymously.
Man up. Don't be a coward. I like to think that one of the differences, as a generalization, between conservatives and liberals is that conservatives prefer to operate with the facts and liberals are overly emotional.
Too many Liberal policies come from an emotional reaction, in my view.
But in this case, when conservatives start acting like spoiled four-year-olds, it is embarrassing to the team.
For those of you writing that the business should have declined the opportunity to host the political event, put your money where your big mouth is and offer to compensate monetarily any operator that turns down Liberal business.
But I also have a message for liberals. Those of you who wrote that the attacks on the business show the "gang mentality" of the party that Pierre Poilievre is building, to quote one online fool, stop and take a look at your own team.
Liberals have never been shy about airing their hateful rhetoric. If you don't see that you are just not looking - or trying.
I am a conservative telling people claiming to be conservative that there is a mile of difference between disagreeing with or even attacking policy and partisan political action; the deliberate attempt to damage the business of a person who merely opened his doors to everyone.
The reality is that he would also welcome you.
Durhamregion.com quotes owner Chris Aucoin: "Not one of these keyboard warriors, or whatever you call them, has come into my store and said, 'Chris, you're an ass----,'nobody has come in and said that. If somebody came in and said they disagreed with what I did, I'd say, 'Yeah sure, you want a beer?'" Would you be gracious enough to accept it? I thought conservatives believed in personal responsibility.
As I said, some of you conservatives need to show some personal control and responsibility. Grow up.
Stop acting like liberals.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Document 423 of 453

$176.3M for city housing; Ottawa gets its cut of $4B in federal cash

Author: Crawford, Blair

Publication info: Crawford, Blair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/176-3m-city-housing-ottawa-gets-cut-4b-federal/docview/2925767659/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%24176.3M+for+city+housing%3B+Ottawa+gets+its+cut+of+%244B+in+federal+cash&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Crawford%2C+Blair&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Ottawa is getting a $176.3-million infusion for affordable homes as its share of the $4-billion federal housing accelerator fund.
The money, which comes after months of negotiations between the city and the federal government, will speed the construction of some 4,400 new homes over the next three years and more than 32,000 in total over the next decade.
The much-anticipated announcement came Monday at Mikinak, a three-building Ottawa Community Housing complex in Wateridge Village on the site of the former Rockcliffe airbase in the city's east end.
Paving the way for the money is the city's agreement to change its zoning regulation to allow four units to be built on any property in the city.
The deal for Ottawa commits the city to introduce a draft bylaw allowing four units as-of-right per lot by June, with final approval of the bylaw in 2025.
It would also increase housing density around transit stations, speed the conversion of commercial office buildings to housing, cut red tape and streamline approval processes.
"Business as usual will not solve the drastic housing challenges that we face today," said Jenna Sudds, the minister of families, children and social development, MP for Kanata-Carleton and a former Ottawa city councillor. Sudds made the announcement on behalf of federal housing minister Sean Fraser.
The agreement brings "significant, lasting changes" to the way housing will be built in the city, she said.
When the city first applied for the money from the fund in 2022, it asked for about $150 million. Mayor Mark Sutcliffe credited city staff for negotiating for even more federal money. He called it "a down payment on getting things built faster."
"This funding won't solve the housing crisis on its own, but it is a giant leap forward for our city and allows Ottawa to take bold and immediate actions to mitigate the housing crisis, build more homes and build faster," Sutcliffe said.
"Our job at the City of Ottawa, and the with support of our federal partners, is to make it as easy as possible to build as fast as possible," Sutcliffe said. "And then it's up to the community, the development community and home buyers, to take it from there."
The "vast majority" of the $176.3 million is earmarked for building more homes "and more affordable homes in particular," he said.
Getting the federal government involved in the housing market to help co-ordinate the provincial and municipal governments is a vital step, said Jason Burggraaf, executive director of the Greater Ottawa Home Builders' Association.
"Obviously, the big piece is the four units per lot. We've seen that been the centrepiece of most agreements that have been reached across Canada. It's really going to help the city meet its intensification targets," Burggraaf said.
The city has set a goal of building 90,000 to 100,000 new homes over the next 20 years, half of that coming from increased density along transit lines and the other half from construction in existing neighbourhoods, he said.
"If you want to get 45,000 or 50,000 new homes in those neighbourhoods, you're talking about converting those single family lots into three or four multiple family units. You have to have the zoning in place to get that density."
In addition, the federal and provincial governments have agreed to eliminate the HST on purpose-built rental units that meet the four-perlot threshold, he said.
"Now you've got a fiscal policy, the housing policy and the zoning you need to build that 'missing middle'of the housing sector," he said.
Increasing density is a politically sensitive issue, with frequent opposition from residents in older, established neighbourhoods with single family homes - the so-called R1 zoning - opposed to the arrival of large, denser multi-unit buildings. But Ontario's controversial Bill 23 outlawed such single-family home zoning and Fraser has made it clear that municipalities that don't comply with the federal goal of four units per lot will be shut out from the accelerator fund.
Earlier this year, the city of Windsor had its $70-million accelerator fund proposal rejected because council had approved four units per lot in only some areas of the city. Windsor Mayor Drew Dilkens said at the time the city wanted to protect its older neighbourhoods from excess densities and that was more important than the federal money.
In contrast, former Mississauga mayor Bonnie Crosbie last fall invoked the controversial "strong mayor powers" afforded by the province to approve fourplexes over her city council's objections. That paved the way for the city to get $112.9 million in accelorator funding.
Crosbie then resigned to become leader of the provincial Liberals.
Sudds was asked Monday what would happen if Ottawa council can't come to an agreement on higher densities.
"Not to negotiate in public, but I think we're in a very good place," she replied. "The city has done an incredible amount of work to get to this point. We've reached an agreement that we have confidence in."
The federal housing accelerator fund will create 100,000 new homes in Canada over the next three years and more than half a million over the next decade, she said.

Document 424 of 453

Nobody on call; Pandemic funding cuts mean fewer emergency doctors

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/nobody-on-call-pandemic-funding-cuts-mean-fewer/docview/2924816304/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Nobody+on+call%3B+Pandemic+funding+cuts+mean+fewer+emergency+doctors&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Queensway Carleton Hospital, which describes its emergency department as one of the busiest in Ontario, has fewer physicians on duty there as of this week, and emergency doctor hours will be cut further in April, officials say.
The cuts are the result of the provincial government winding down special pandemic funding.
The situation, in the midst of a widespread emergency department crisis, is causing alarm.
"It means we are not going to be able to provide as many physician hours of coverage and care. It means we have to cut back shifts," said Dr. Michael Herman, an emergency physician at the hospital who is a spokesperson for the Canadian Association of Emergency Physicians.
"Canada-wide, we see the state of emergency departments. We see people waiting hours on end, and we hear about the harms that are coming from people waiting hours and hours. Cutting these hours is only going to exacerbate the problem."
The funding cuts are part of the Ontario government's move to end special pandemic funding for hospitals.
During the pandemic, the government increased the number of emergency physician hours it would pay for by 20 per cent.
It began to gradually reduce those physician hours starting last April.
Because physician emergency department staffing is based on volumes, the cuts weren't felt at Queensway Carleton until now.
Last week, the hospital learned the province would fund four fewer hours a day of emergency physician coverage than it had been supporting.
As of April, there will be 10 fewer hours a day of physician coverage in the hospital's emergency department, a hospital spokesperson said.
Although that suggests volumes in the emergency department are now closer to what they were pre-pandemic, Herman says that is misleading and that it will worsen wait times in emergency departments.
"I think anyone who has been in an emergency department knows that quote-unquote lower volumes are still really high and we are certainly not in the pre-pandemic world anymore," he said.
Herman says the acuity and complexity of patients coming to the emergency department has changed in recent years, in part because many of them no longer have primary caregivers. That means many required more time than the current funding formula allows for.
That funding model for determining how many emergency physicians are required is based on an old study, he said.
It is currently being updated by the province.
He also notes that the funding model does not count patients who leave without being treated, a growing problem as emergency department waits increase.
Over the weekend, the hospital posted an advisory on its website asking people who were coming to the emergency department to be prepared to wait. "Please be patient as we experience longer than usual wait times in our ED due to patient volumes." Emergency departments across Ontario and Canada have been experiencing historically long wait times for care due, in part, to nursing shortages, shortages of hospital beds and increasingly complex patient needs. Reducing the number of physicians working will make the situation worse, Herman said.
This past week, Premier Doug Ford and Prime Minister Justin Trudeau signed a $3.1-billion health funding deal under which Ontario will hire more health workers. During a media conference, Ford suggested stretched emergency departments would be on his radar.
"I'm going to be zoned in on these emergency departments," he said in response to a question about ballooning emergency wait times.
In a statement, a spokesperson for Health Minister Sylvia Jones said the additional funding for emergency physicians "was part of temporary, extraordinary measures funding given to hospitals during the COVID-19 pandemic to reimburse them for the costs incurred as a result of patient surge due to the global pandemic. Hospitals across the province received this temporary reimbursement funding and were notified well in advance of when this temporary funding would be wound down."
Herman says the timing to roll back the funding for emergency doctors doesn't make sense.
"I think our volumes and wait times reflect that this is absolutely necessary (physician) coverage. This is coverage that is keeping our head above water with us paddling as fast as we can," he said.
"For all the talk of investing in health care, this sure feels like the opposite."

Subject: Hospitals; Emergency medical care; Funding cuts; Departments; Pandemics; Physicians

Business indexing term: Subject: Hospitals Physicians

Document 425 of 453

Belleville begs for aid fighting ODs

Author: Baldwin, Derek

Publication info: Baldwin, Derek. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/belleville-begs-aid-fighting-ods/docview/2924816181/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Belleville+begs+for+aid+fighting+ODs&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-11&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.2&amp;au=Baldwin%2C+Derek&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Mayor Neil Ellis declared a state of emergency in the city Thursday to free up more funding from senior governments to tackle an escalating homelessness, mental health and drug and overdose crisis in the city.
The declaration followed first responders attending five more drug overdose calls Wednesday adding to a disturbing string of multiple overdoses on Tuesday, when 17 people overdosed in a 24-hour period.
Belleville Police reported the latest five overdoses happened between 10 a.m. and midnight in the Bridge Street Church drop-in centre area in the city core and all persons were transported to Belleville General Hospital for treatment.
The crisis has attracted national media attention focusing on a slew of overdoses attributed to tainted street drugs believed to be mixed with tranquilizers that have more devastating impacts on drug users.
"The past two days have exemplified just how critical the addiction, mental health and homelessness crisis has become in our community," Ellis said. "We, as a city, know that we are at the point where doing our best doesn't cut it anymore. Our emergency services, health care system and municipal resources are being stretched to the very limits and we are close to a breaking point."
"We need serious action and support from senior level government to deal with this crisis and until we begin to see meaningful discussions on how to address the matter, I fear nothing will change. That is why we are calling on the provincial and federal government for support. I urge our local municipal partners facing the same issues to do the same."
On Friday, Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford pledged support for Belleville.
"We're going to be working with the tools that we have at the federal level. I mentioned it to Doug as well that I spoke with Neil this morning and there is work the federal government can do to support the provinces as we move forward on more treatment and recovery centres understanding that this phenomenon of toxic drug supply is not just hitting our big cities, they're hitting small towns and communities across Ontario, right across the country," Trudeau said.

Subject: Drug overdose; Mental health

Trudeau, Ford sign $3.1B health deal

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-ford-sign-3-1b-health-deal/docview/2924113538/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%2C+Ford+sign+%243.1B+health+deal&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-09&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.17&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Premier Doug Ford will announce details Friday of a $3.1-billion infusion aimed at improving health care, according to a report. The federal and Ontario leaders will meet in King City, north of Toronto, to announce the deal, the Toronto Star reported Thursday night. In exchange for the new cash, the Ford government would commit spending to establish new primary care teams aimed at boosting access to family doctors, the outlet reported. The province reportedly said it would add hundreds of new family physicians and nurse practitioners along with thousands of new nurses and personal support workers. Enrolment in health-care education programs will increase by 700, with a specific allotment for northern Ontario. Some of the money will be aimed at increasing youth mental health services and substance abuse services in rural, remote and Indigenous communities. - Sun staff

Document 427 of 453

Smith draws a crowd; Protesters rally for trans youth during Alberta premier's trip to Ottawa

Author: Glass, Marlo

Publication info: Glass, Marlo. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-draws-crowd-protesters-rally-trans-youth/docview/2923167310/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+draws+a+crowd%3B+Protesters+rally+for+trans+youth+during+Alberta+premier%27s+trip+to+Ottawa&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Glass%2C+Marlo&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Protesters gathered in downtown Ottawa on Monday to show support for transgender youth in light of Alberta Premier Danielle Smith's visit to the capital.
Last week, Smith announced that her United Conservative Party government would ban puberty blockers and hormone therapy for children 15 and younger who have not already begun those treatments. Her government also plans to require parental consent for kids 15 and under to go by a different name or pronoun at school. Teenagers aged 16-17 would not need permission, but schools would have to notify their guardians.
Gathering across from Parliament Hill, rally-goers chanted, "Trans rights are human rights," while a small group of counter-protesters held signs that read, "Thanks Danielle," and "Leave the kids alone."
Holly Brown attended the rally as a mother of a gender-diverse child, carrying a sign that read, "Blockers are saving my kid's life."
"There's not a question in my mind," she said, adding that hormone therapy, which delays physical changes brought on by puberty, should be accessible to all.
"If this came to Ontario tomorrow, my child's life would immediately be put at risk," Brown said.
While the Canadian Pediatrics Society says hormone blockers should not be prescribed before puberty, access to hormone blockers has been associated with lowering the risk of suicidal thoughts.
Fae Johnstone, one of the organizers of the rally and executive director of the Society for Queer Momentum, said the rally was joining parents, young people and advocates across the city, "speaking up against Danielle Smith's dangerous attacks on the rights of trans kids."
Smith's proposed legislation is the latest in a wave of policies dictating how trans and non-binary students can identify in schools. Last summer, New Brunswick's provincial government made it mandatory for trans and non-binary students under 16 to obtain parental consent in order for their preferred names and pronouns to be used by teachers. Fellow Progressive Conservative Premier Scott Moe followed suit with a similar consent policy in Saskatchewan.
"It's not just kids, it's about rolling back social progress while we fight for a more free and equal future," Johnstone said. "So we've got to show up in numbers."
L iberal Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault told reporters earlier Monday he requested a meeting with Smith to discuss her proposed changes.
Boissonnault, who is openly gay and the only Liberal cabinet minister from Alberta, said Smith is pushing "draconian" measures on some of the province's most vulnerable youth.
Smith was in town Monday to open a new provincial office in Ottawa, and was also expected to give a luncheon speech to members of the Economic Club of Canada.
- With files from The Canadian Press

Subject: Non-binary gender; Puberty; Endocrine therapy; Consent

Document 428 of 453

Montreal a 'parking lot for thieves'

Author: Bruemmer, Rene

Publication info: Bruemmer, Rene. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/montreal-parking-lot-thieves/docview/2923167303/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Montreal+a+%27parking+lot+for+thieves%27&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-07&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Bruemmer%2C+Rene&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Standing before a backdrop of shipping containers awaiting departure in the Port of Montreal, federal Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre held a campaign-style news conference Tuesday pledging to increase security measures to tackle a countrywide surge in car thefts.
Flanked by posters reading "stop the crime" and "stop car theft," Poilievre said that, "after eight years of Justin Trudeau," Canadian vehicles are being stolen at historically high rates.
Many are being shipped overseas from Montreal's port, which Poilievre dubbed "a parking lot for thieves."
If elected in 2025, Poilievre promised he'd invest in 24 X-ray scanners at a cost of $55 million for the country's major federal ports in Montreal, Vancouver, Halifax and Prince Rupert, B.C., and hire a special team of 75 Canada Border Services Agency officers to run the scanners "and catch stolen cars before they leave our country."
Thirty of those agents would be placed in Montreal.
To finance the new hires, Poilievre said he'd "fire useless management consultants at CBSA."
"Common-sense Conservatives will reverse Trudeau's reckless policies that have turned our federal ports into parking lots for stolen cars," he said.
His plan came days before the federal Liberals are set to hold a national summit on combating auto theft with police, border agents and industry executives.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada says auto theft has become a national crisis, with more than 200 vehicles stolen on average every day.
In the first half of last year, auto theft jumped 31% in Ontario and 17% in Quebec.
That came after a 50% jump in both provinces from 2021 to '22.
More than 9,500 cars were stolen in Montreal in 2022, and that figure was surpassed in '23.
The problem is adding a further financial burden to cash-strapped Canadians, Poilievre said.
Car theft claims in Ontario were up 329% in the first half of 2023, totalling $700 million in losses for insurance firms in Canada.
The Insurance Bureau of Canada estimates this costs every driver in Ontario an extra $130 a year.

Subject: Automobile theft; Robbery

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Document 429 of 453

Smith goes to Ottawa; Alberta premier defends proposed transgender policy reforms as Liberals cry foul

Author: Taylor, Stephanie

Publication info: Taylor, Stephanie. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/smith-goes-ottawa-alberta-premier-defends/docview/2922687890/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Smith+goes+to+Ottawa%3B+Alberta+premier+defends+proposed+transgender+policy+reforms+as+Liberals+cry+foul&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-06&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Taylor%2C+Stephanie&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Alberta Premier Danielle Smith defended her controversial new suite of proposed transgender youth policies Monday in Ottawa as federal Liberals accused her of pushing an anti-LGBTQ agenda.
Smith announced last week that her United Conservative Party government would ban puberty blockers and hormone therapy for children 15 and younger who have not already begun those treatments.
Her government also plans to require parental consent for kids 15 and under to go by a different name or pronoun at school. Teenagers aged 16-17 would not need permission, but schools would have to notify their guardians.
The policies have spurred several days of protest.
There is no "single voice" that can speak on behalf of the entire transgender community, Smith suggested during an event in the national capital to mark the opening of a new provincial-federal liaison office.
She said has spoken to some transgender people who expressed concerns about children's ability to transition.
Smith defended the package of proposals as part of a "considered approach" she wants to take when it comes to children with diverse gender identities.
The goal is to ensure children are "fully informed" about the decisions they are making in the event they may regret them later in life, she added.
"We had to have a conversation about what is the appropriate ages to be able to make those life-altering decisions."
Earlier in the day, Liberal Employment Minister Randy Boissonnault said he requested a meeting with Smith to discuss the proposed changes.
Boissonnault, who is openly gay and the only Liberal cabinet minister from Alberta, said Smith is pushing "draconian" measures on some of the province's most vulnerable youth.
After s ome back-and-forth between their respective offices, the pair had been expected to meet later Monday.
The federal government's ability to ask for a Supreme Court reference on any use of the notwithstanding clause by provinces is an "important legal tool," Boissonnault noted.
"We will be playing very close attention to see what's in Premier Smith's legislation, which I hope never makes it to the floor of the (legislature)," he said.
Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre, asked about the proposals during a news conference Monday in Brampton, Ont., said: "Let parents raise kids and provinces run schools and hospitals."
Poilievre did not specifically address Smith's proposals - notable, since his office told MPs last week to "refrain" from speaking about Alberta's specific measures, and to "flag" any related media requests that come in.
In Toronto, Ontario Premier Doug Ford signalled that his government had no plans to follow Alberta's lead. "No. We have a law here and we're leaving everything alone," he said, echoing previous remarks from the province's health minister.
Local advocates in Ottawa, meanwhile, were organizing an "emergency" protest downtown later Monday in light of Smith's visit to the capital.
The Alberta government office Smith opened in Ottawa is intended to help advance the province's priorities and fortify federal-provincial ties, the Alberta government said in a news release.
It said the office would "help create stronger relationships with governments in Ottawa and across Canada while increasing the province's advocacy on matters of importance to Albertans."

Subject: Transgender persons

Document 430 of 453

YOU SAID IT: In praise of Danielle Smith

Author: Menary, Doug

Publication info: Menary, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-said-praise-danielle-smith/docview/2921649172/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=YOU+SAID+IT%3A+In+praise+of+Danielle+Smith&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Menary%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: IN PRAISE OF DANIELLE
Alberta Premier Danielle Smith is an inspiration to all Canadians who believe in freedom across this once-great country.
Now battered and bruised by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau since 2015 with his radical, economy-killing green policies, we are going down the drain. However, there is a heroine who has stepped into the fray and has decided to fight the fire-breathing Ottawa dragon. Smith will have none of that insane carbon tax and zero emissions folly that will destroy this country.
She has been helped along by Saskatchewan Premier Moe, but sadly Ontario, under the stewardship of Premier Doug Ford, has been eerily silent, with the former Toronto councillor deciding to cosy up to Trudeau rather than taking a brave stand.
We need Danielle Smiths in Canada. Pierre Poilievre is cut somewhat from that cloth, but he is in opposition and will be for a good year and a half.
Smith has to prevail or we will become a poverty-stricken, green, tinpot dictatorship.
STEPHEN FLANAGAN
OTTAWA
PERFECT TIMING
Re: Former justice minister David Lametti resigns as Montreal MP to join law firm, online, Jan. 25
It likely is no coincidence that MP David Lametti resigned mere days after the Federal Court ruled that the Trudeau government lacked the grounds to impose the Emergencies Act to break up the convoy protest. After all, it was Lametti who helped bring in the EA as justice minister.
Let's not forget Lametti got the job after justice minister Jody Wilson-Raybould allegedly refused to give SNC-Lavalin the deferred prosecution deal Prime Minister Justin Trudeau wanted.
Lametti likely also was not happy being relegated to the back benches, becoming a victim of Trudeau's DEI (diversity, equity and inclusion) policy.
LARRY COMEAU
OTTAWA
IN LEFTIES' SIGHTS
So the governing body of psychologists in Ontario has decided to make Dr. Jordan Peterson a scapegoat for all those who dare go against their beliefs.
Apparently in this country we are not allowed to comment or question the lefties' view of the world.
Careful Jordan: As you well know, the woke crowd have you in their sights.
To them, you are the modern-day equivalent of Dracula, and must be destroyed for daring to question their science and beliefs.
WAYNE BOYCE
CARLETON PLACE
HAVE YOUR SAY
Your letters are welcome, at: OttSun.Oped@sunmedia.ca . Include your first and last name AND city/town. Keep your letters short - and please try to be civil, even when criticizing or disagreeing. We edit for accuracy, length, clarity and legal concerns.

YOU SAID IT: Two choices to lead

In praise of danielle; Letters to the editor

Preem: You're going to jail; Crime so bad, Ford's pals have a bat by the bed

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/preem-youre-going-jail-crime-so-bad-fords-pals/docview/2921316268/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Preem%3A+You%27re+going+to+jail%3B+Crime+so+bad%2C+Ford%27s+pals+have+a+bat+by+the+bed&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-02-02&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Premier Doug Ford's warning to criminals that he's "coming after" them didn't stop a group of thugs from smashing and grabbing their way through a Toronto mall Wednesday night.
Criminals don't care what politicians say because they know who's in charge of the streets. It's them.
Toronto Police say four masked men, described as about 5 feet 8, stormed a jewelry store in the Yorkdale Shopping Centre, near Dufferin St., around 8:45 p.m.
One of the bandits "produced a handgun and pointed it at employees" while the others "began smashing the glass of display cases with hammers," stealing jewelry and cash, before they all drove off in a newer-model grey SUV.
But don't worry, the premier has strong words for such criminals.
"We're going to catch you and you're going to jail," Ford said Wednesday. "We're going to keep you in He knows there are not enough jail cells to incarcerate every criminal and the courts are backlogged for years.
That means the boss is not Ford or Prime Minister Justin Trudeau. It's these gangs that are in charge.
They will carjack you or steal your car from your driveway, or kick down the front door of your home without fear of getting caught. Even if they are nabbed, they know they will be released from custody within a few days.
The premier knows how bad it is out there.
"I've talked to friends that are sleeping with baseball bats beside their bed because someone's going to kick the door in and put a gun to their head and say, hand over your keys," Ford said.
What kind of province have we become when the premier's friends sleep with bats? Just imagine those without such powerful friends. What are they supposed to do? The public has become sitting ducks while leaders oversee the destruction caused by drugs, poverty, gambling debts, mental health and a focus on progressive wokeism.
While Ford recommended victims of crime wait for police instead of using on a bat, he didn't mention the average emergency response time is 22 minutes.
The idea of people defending themselves with a baseball bat is interesting since just last month a 22-year-old international student working at a Peterborough corner store was slugged with one by a crook during a holdup.
In that case, the clerk managed to snatch the bat away from the robber and deliver a retaliatory blow.
The innocent kid, who is not friends with the premier, ended up charged with aggravated assault for defending himself. He has no money, no job, he's under house arrest with strict bail conditions and may have to wait two years to try to clear his name.
The criminals must be laughing their heads off. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Subject: Jewelry stores; Robbery; Criminals

Business indexing term: Subject: Jewelry stores

YOU SAID IT: Two choices to lead

Author: Menary, Doug

Publication info: Menary, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-said-two-choices-lead/docview/2921077372/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=unknown&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=YOU+SAID+IT%3A+Two+choices+to+lead&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;issn=&amp;date=2024-02-01&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=&amp;au=Menary%2C+Doug&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun+%28Online%29&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: TWO CHOICES TO LEAD
Re: Who would replace him? Letter, online, Jan. 19
I read with interest your article about who is available to replace Justin Trudeau as Liberal leader. As you wrote, there isn't a particularly strong leadership candidate in the Liberal party. May I suggest two leaders who I would support to lead the Liberal party in the next election.
Jean Charest: He has the credentials to lead the Liberal party, with a lean towards more fiscal responsibility. He is fluent in both official languages and carried the left-centre of Conservative populism in that leadership contest. I supported his bid during his campaign, which offered the direction I would like to see our country follow. The Conservative party is often referred to as a collection of rednecks. Jean Charest would be able to lead the majority of Canadians who are not particularly enthused about Poilievre.
Tom Mulcair: A respectable opinion columnist with CTV broadcasting and the Montreal Gazette, who is well enough known to be an influence on a federal election outcome. Like Ed Broadbent before him, Tom was well enough liked as a leader, but the NDP party was not a viable alternative in his time period.
With the support of the media, a groundswell of support might install a leader of our country who we could respect going forward.
LARRY ROBERTS
SURREY, B.C.
CBC LITE
Has anybody else noticed how CTV has become CBC lite? All its shows on the "news" channel are carefully constructed to bash Conservatives and Republicans. Trump is bad and Poilievre is Trump North. They have people saying how bad it was under Trump.
But: No war in Ukraine. No war in Israel. The Taliban weren't in charge in Afghanistan. The economy was starting to boom. Kim Jong Un was quiet and playing with his rockets at home. Iran was quiet and broke. In Canada, interest rates were low. Houses were affordable. The carbon tax was nowhere to be seen. Jobs were available. I would go back to that "Trump is Bad" time enthusiastically.
ANDY ABBOTT
HAWKESBURY
TUCKER FOR PM
Tucker Carlson for prime minister. He had the most popular show on cable news when he became a little too opinionated for the Murdoch clan over at Fox News and they canned him. However, his dismissal was the best thing that ever happened and his popularity has since soared to incredible heights. His speech in Alberta ruffled a few feathers, especially those of political pundits and those in the mainstream media who ordinarily toe the progressive line. The man is not afraid to ridicule Justin Trudeau. We badly need a Tucker type in this country.
STEPHEN FLANAGAN
OTTAWA

Document 434 of 453

Carbon tax won't save the Liberals

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/carbon-tax-wont-save-liberals/docview/2920380262/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Carbon+tax+won%27t+save+the+Liberals&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Trudeau Liberals keep clinging to the idea, borne of their political desperation, that if they can just properly explain their carbon tax to Canadians, they'll flock to it in support.
Their problem is they had one chance to make a good first impression about their carbon tax and they blew it.
It started with the Liberal claim when they imposed their carbon tax in 2019 that they had found a magical tax leaving 80% of households paying it better off.
That claim was blown apart in March 2022 by independent, non-partisan Parliamentary Budget Officer Yves Giroux, who reported that after you factored in the negative impact of the carbon tax on the economy, most households - 60% - ended up worse off.
How much worse? The PBO estimated in Alberta the average net cost per household, factoring in quarterly climate action incentive rebates, will be $710 this year, rising to $2,773 in 2030.
Here are the PBO's estimated costs for the other provinces - the first figure being the estimated average household cost this year, the second in 2030: Ontario: $478, $1,820; Saskatchewan $410, $1723; Manitoba $386, $1490; Nova Scotia $431, $1,513; P.E.I. $465, $1,521; Newfoundland and Labrador $347, $1,316.
(New Brunswick joined the federal carbon tax system on July 1 last year - after the PBO provided these estimates - while Quebec and B.C. have provincial carbon pricing plans, approved by the federal government.)
The Trudeau Liberals also said paying the carbon tax would lower Canada's industrial greenhouse gas emissions but after a significant drop during the first year of the pandemic in 2020 - not because of the carbon tax but because of the global economic slowdown caused by COVID-19 - emissions rose again in 2021.
We won't know Canada's emissions for 2022 until this April because the feds always report them two years after the fact, but the Canadian Climate Institute estimates Canada's emissions went up, compared to 2021.
Finally, there's the Liberal myth that even if we were to hit their emission targets for 2030 and 2050 - we're far behind both - the weather in Canada would somehow magically improve.
In fact, as the PBO noted, Canada's emissions at 1.5% of the global total "are not large enough to materially impact climate change."

Subject: Emissions; Carbon; Households; Climate change; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: Canada

Document 435 of 453

Gotta build somewhere; Ottawa overhauling zoning bylaws

Author: Crawford, Blair

Publication info: Crawford, Blair. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/gotta-build-somewhere-ottawa-overhauling-zoning/docview/2920360707/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Gotta+build+somewhere%3B+Ottawa+overhauling+zoning+bylaws&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-31&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Crawford%2C+Blair&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The City of Ottawa is about to begin the largest overhaul of its zoning bylaws in a quarter-century, one that will profoundly change the way the city builds and grows.
And, if the words "zoning bylaw" makes you zone out, you shouldn't. The impact of the changes will be felt from Kinburn to Cumberland, from Manotick to Mechanicsville. It will set the ground rules for everything from residential density to building heights to how much street parking is needed.
Work begins on the Comprehensive Zoning Bylaw Amendment in March and likely won't be complete until the end of 2025. It's the first major revision to bylaws since amalgamation in 2001 and may be the single biggest task of Mayor Mark Sutcliffe and the current term of council.
"It seems geeky, but this is going to affect everyone," said Kitchissippi Coun. Jeff Leiper, chair of the city's planning and housing committee. "It's the thing I'm probably the most excited about."
The zoning revamp will open up new areas of Ottawa to infill development, which until now has been largely concentrated in and around the downtown core.
"Kitchissippi has been a development hot spot for a couple of decades now, and that's coming everywhere else," Leiper said. "The building boom is going to slow down in my ward as the land values have skyrocketed, but the same dynamic that's affected Westboro and Hintonburg is coming to Carlington and Bay Ward and Overbrook. It's going to come to Orléans."
At its core is the move by Ottawa and cities across Canada to end what's known as "exclusionary zoning" - the traditional post-war suburb with single-family homes and broad green lawns. Those idyllic Leave it to Beaver communities are car-dependent, wasteful of space and expensive to service. It's labelled "exclusionary" because the cost of housing there puts those neighbourhoods out of the reach of low-income families.
In Ottawa, those areas are known as R1 zoning. The federal government has made it clear, and the Ontario government has made it law, that exclusionary zoning must end. Ottawa's official plan, passed in 2022, effectively ended R1 zoning. Premier Doug Ford's controversial Bill 23, the More Homes Built Faster Act, directs all Ontario municipalities to allow three or more residential units per building lot.
Federal housing minister Sean Fraser has also made it known that money from the $4-billion federal housing accelerator fund will only go to municipalities that agree to end exclusionary zoning. (Sutcliffe announced this past week that Ottawa had reached a deal to receive accelerator funds, much of which would be earmarked for affordable housing. The city requested $150 million, but the exact amount it will receive has not been announced).
"The province passed legislation that requires municipalities to allow three units allows three units on any lot," Leiper said. "But, as far as I'm concerned, it's still a bit of a creaky bylaw. It was a quick response to a directive."
Ottawa's zoning bylaws still contain rules about things like setbacks from property lines and building heights that are counter to the increased density goals of the official plan. Squeezing three or more units on one lot is difficult if the bylaw says you can only build so high and must be a certain number of metres from property lines.
"The developers are looking at our most recent official plan that really doubles down on intensification and saying, 'These are the kinds of buildings that our official plan tells us we should be able to build. Why doesn't the zoning reflect that?'" L eiper "So getting the zoning up to date is critical to provide the kind of orderly development according to the vision council has adopted," Leiper added. "The process of getting a rezoning is complex. It's expensive. It's time-consuming. One of the most important things we can do as a city to address the housing crisis is to be sure that the as-of-right permissions are in place to allow builders to build housing rather than seek regulatory approvals."
Many of those disputes end up before the Ontario Land Tribunal, an expensive and time-consuming process that can further slow home construction and add costs.
Developers, of course, are eager for the coming clarity.
"We're hoping there's going to be a lot more simplicity," said Jason Burggraaf, executive director of the Greater Ottawa Home Builders' Association. "There are hundreds of different zoning labels. We think of R1, R2, R3, R4 and R5, but there are dozens and dozens of little appendices they add on: R1aa and R1ab ... There's a tremendous variations for every type of conceivable change for lot size and location and particularity.
It's so gargantuan and hard to navigate."
Some of the zoning bylaws are leftovers from pre-amalgamation days. Burggraaf said one developer was stymied when trying to build an apartment in an area designated as R4, which allows low rises of up to four storeys.
"There was a rule that said, 'R4 apartments, but no senior residences.' Why? Why ban that type of use? It could have been instituted decades ago because someone didn't want a seniors'residence near their house and it just got carried over the years and through amalgamation. It's those weird kind of particularities that we have to clean out."
That Ottawa has a housing crisis is no secret. Council declared a "housing emergency" in 2020. The wait list for a place to live with Ottawa Community Housing is 12,000 names long and the average wait time is more than five years.

Subject: Bylaws; Councils; Zoning

Identifier / keyword: ottawa; begin; largest; overhaul; zoning; bylaws

Document 436 of 453

Trumped-up?; Poll shows strategy to campaign on prez hopeful could backfire on Grits

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trumped-up-poll-shows-strategy-campaign-on-prez/docview/2919761472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trumped-up%3F%3B+Poll+shows+strategy+to+campaign+on+prez+hopeful+could+backfire+on+Grits&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.7&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Trying to tie Conservative Leader Pierre Poilievre to Donald Trump may not be the trump card the Trudeau Liberals think it is. For months, the Liberals have been trying to hurt Poilievre's support among voters by connecting him to the presumptive Republican candidate.
A new poll from Abacus Data shows that about 40% of voters believe Poilievre and Trump are similar, while roughly 33% find them different, with the rest saying they don't know.
More importantly, though, is that more Canadians believe Poilievre is the best option to deal with Trump if he wins the White House for a second time in November.
When asked, "If Donald Trump is the next U.S. President, would it be better for Canada if Justin Trudeau or Pierre Poilievre were Prime Minister?" a full 44% chose Poilievre, compared to just 29% picking Trudeau.
That includes 17% of current Liberal voters believing Poilievre would be the better choice and 23% of current NDP voters.
Abacus Data CEO David Coletto says the strategy of linking Poilievre and Trump could work for the Liberals, but they have their own problems.
"It may also be moot so long as people dislike Mr. Trudeau as much as they do and the desire for change is as strong as it is," Coletto said.
In fact, when asked to rate four politicians on a scale of 1 to 10, with one indicating a strong dislike, Donald Trump scored the lowest among Canadians at 3, Justin Trudeau was next at 3.6, Joe Biden was at 4.9 and Pierre Poilievre was at 5.1. That means Canadians dislike Trudeau almost as much as they dislike Trump, which is not a good sign for the Liberal government.
"The public wants change, is cranky and anxious, and is looking for an alternative to Mr. Trudeau and the Liberals," Coletto said.
His survey of voter support found that 54% of Canadians believe Poilievre would make the best prime minister, including 21% of those who voted Liberal in 2021 and 37% of those who voted NDP. By comparison, just 46% chose Trudeau as the best PM in a head to head matchup.
Nationally, Abacus has the Conservatives up 15 points over the Liberals - 40% support to 25% - with the NDP in third place at 20%. More importantly, the Conservatives hold a commanding lead in every province across the country other than Quebec, where they are performing well enough to hold the 10 seats they currently have.
These numbers do not bode well for the Trudeau Liberals and, as a fascinating column by John Ibbitson in the Globe and Mail showed recently, things are about to get worse. If an election isn't called by April 22, the number of seats up for grabs in the next election will rise from 338 to 343.
The redistribution based on the population of the 2021 census - already out of date - means that Alberta would get three new seats, while Ontario and British Columbia would each get an additional seat. In Ontario, Toronto and Northern Ontario each lose a seat, with the 905 suburban area around Toronto getting the new seat and the two others being shifted.
Four of the five seats are going to be difficult for the Liberals to win, with the fifth new seat being a toss-up with the Conservatives.
It's clear the Trudeau Liberals want to campaign against Donald Trump if they can. If the constant push on that front yields results for them, an election called on April 21 would put voting day in late May or early June, just as the American campaign is solidifying.
That's how desperate the Trudeau Liberals are, campaigning against a politician in another country, watching the calendar to see which dates work for them, and praying Canadians are dumb enough to believe their spin.
At this point, my money is still on Canadians being fed up with Trudeau.
Conducted Jan. 18-23, the Abacus Data poll of 2,199 Canadian adults - using a random sample of online panellists - is considered accurate within 2.1%, 19 times out of 20.

Subject: Political campaigns; Canadians; Voters; Prime ministers

People: Trump, Donald J; Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Document 437 of 453

Wiltin'in Milton?; Riding next door to Liberal leader may not be sure thing some expect

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/wiltinin-milton-riding-next-door-liberal-leader/docview/2919760154/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Wiltin%27in+Milton%3F%3B+Riding+next+door+to+Liberal+leader+may+not+be+sure+thing+some+expect&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-29&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.5&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: If Parm Gill is out, does that mean Bonnie Crombie is in? Since Gill announced last week that he would resign as the PC MPP for Milton, and minister of red tape reduction in the Ford government, speculation has turned to Crombie.
Will the former Mississauga mayor and newly minted Liberal leader move the 30 km or so northwest to run in Milton? It seems like the logical thing to do since the move could get Crombie a seat in the legislature, but running in Milton may not be the wisest thing from a couple of perspectives.
First off, if Crombie were successful in winning the seat and taking the riding from the PC party, she would have a steady presence at Queen's Park, which is a good thing. But it would also tie her to the legislature, which may not be a good thing.
With the Ontario Liberal Party a shadow of its former self, the party seriously needs to reorganize and rebuild - tasks that might be easier for Crombie to accomplish away from Queen's Park.
Sure, with a seat in the legislature, Crombie could have easier access to the media covering the daily ups and downs of Queen's Park, but in truth, she has easy media access as it is. Rebuilding the infrastructure on the ground that's required to win an election is equally important to winning a seat - if not more so - for a party that wants to regain power.
The second reason Crombie may want to think twice about running is she might not win.
Milton is a swing riding that - in its current form and its previous incarnations - has gone back and forth between the Liberals and PCs provincially for years. The same is true for the riding's federal counterpart, which is held by Liberal MP Adam van Koeverden.
In the last provincial election, Liberal candidate Sameera Ali took 38% of the vote to Gill's 43%. Gill slightly outperformed his party's provincewide level of voter support - 41% - while Ali far outperformed the 24% the Liberals took across Ontario.
While there is no doubt Crombie would have a shot at winning the riding, what is best for her party? Concerns that she's not from the area and would be seen as an outsider should be ignored by anyone making this decision - van Koeverden won Milton federally in 2019 after moving from downtown Toronto. If Crombie were running in Lambton-Kent-Middlesex, that might be an issue, but not Milton.
There was a time when leaders without a seat in the legislature would effectively be given a pass by the other parties when contesting a byelection. Those days are long gone, so we shouldn't expect the PC party to go easy on Crombie if she decides to contest Milton.
In 2009, when John Tory sought to win a seat in the legislature as PC leader, the Liberals refused to go easy on him and took the riding of Haliburton-Kawartha Lakes-Brock. In 2015, newly minted PC leader Patrick Brown ran in Simcoe-North and won despite a hard-fought campaign by the Liberals.
Given that record, there is no reason for the PCs to go easy on Crombie.
The PC party has held Milton since 2018, and they won't want to give it up soon, meaning Premier Doug Ford is likely to fight hard in this byelection. Keeping it, if Crombie enters, will be a difficult task and require a strong local candidate who can go toe to toe with the new Liberal leader.
The NDP is effectively nonexistent in the riding, taking less than 10% of the vote in the last election, which makes this a PC-Liberal battle.
There are a lot of options for both parties to consider between now and when the byelection is called.

Subject: Provincial elections; Legislatures

Identifier / keyword: bonnie; crombie; announced; resign; milton; minister
