Document 438 of 453

Snip snip; Cuts to international student visas could hit Ontario schools hard

Author: Warren, Ken

Publication info: Warren, Ken. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/snip-cuts-international-student-visas-could-hit/docview/2918648492/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Snip+snip%3B+Cuts+to+international+student+visas+could+hit+Ontario+schools+hard&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-26&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Warren%2C+Ken&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: WhenMohamedFaris was exploring global options to pursue a mechanical engineering degree five years ago, he embraced the opportunity that came with coming to Carleton University.
"Canada was a safe haven, giving me an ability to be myself within a Canadian environment," said Faris, a native of India who grew up in Dubai. "Carleton was a beautiful campus in the capital city. It was a good program. It checked all the boxes."
While spending more than $50,000 annually in tuition and living expenses, Faris has since become fully immersed in the Ottawa university experience.
He lived in residence in his first year. When COVID-19 lockdowns were in place, he studied from Dubai via Zoom. During the past few years, he has also become deeply involved in campus political life, climbing the ranks to become the first South Asian president of the Carleton University Student Association in 2023-24. He says he would "absolutely" be interested in working in Canada once his degree is complete.
All of that gives him a unique perspective on the federal government's decision this week to cut 35% of the visas given to out-of-countr y post-secondary students - down to 364,000 - for the next two years.
There are currently more than 807,000 international students in Canada, according to the Canadian Bureau for International Education, but an influx of immigration, coupled with a shortage of affordable housing, has created a crisis.
There are no hard numbers yet on where, exactly, the cuts will come, but due to the number of visas typically issued to Ontario schools, they could receive cuts of as much as 50%.
Changing campus diversity Immigration Minister Marc Miller says the reduction is necessary "in order to maintain a sustainable level of temporary residence in Canada."
Faris understands the problem, acknowledging the housing situation has changed significantly since he first arrived in 2019.
But he says a cap on incoming international students is not the best way to deal with the issue and could drastically shake up the post-secondary environment.
Universities Canada says 18% of full-time students in Ontario are international, including 15% of undergraduates.
The University of Ottawa says 22% of its enrolment comes from non-Canadian students, and that number is 34% at Algonquin College. Carleton University did not respond to a request for the percentage of international students at its school.
While Faris is not in danger of losing his visa - the new mandate would be limited to new students and doesn't apply to master's degree or doctoral students - he says the opportunity he received may not be available to someone else.
"At first glance, it seems very punitive to students," said Faris, who advocated against the pending change last fall as part of the Canadian Alliance of Students Associations. "International students bring a lot of different thoughts, diversity and knowledge to a campus. I've talked to a lot of students. I have seen how this unfolded. I actually predicted this might happen, but I thought it might come in five years, not in five months."
Big business
The cut in student visas isn't going over well at the top levels of college and university administrations, either.
"International students enrich our university community and the communities across Canada they settle in after graduation," Jacques Beauvais, Ottawa's inter improvost and vice president of academic affairs, said in a statement to Postmedia. uOttawa also says foreign students are not contributing to the housing crisis here. There was room for all students requiring residence space for 2023-24.
Algonquin College president and CEO Claude Brulé said he wasn't surprised by the announcement, given all the previous speculation.
"In general, my reaction is that this is going to have an impact," Brulé said. "There are limited levers to address are limited levers to address (the housing shortage), but it should be about how (students) are contributing to the labour market and the local economy. We have more than 200 programs of study and some of the programs are very attractive to international students."
The cuts will have a major impact on revenue streams.
In 2019, Premier Doug Ford abandoned the previous Liberal government's free tuition program for lowand middle-income students. He replaced it with a 10% across-the-board cut in tuition and has frozen fees since then.
Meanwhile, the costs for those students from outside Canada have risen dramatically to make up the difference, and the gap is striking.
In 2023-24, annual tuition for an undergrad at Carleton ranged from $6,060 to $10,700 for Canadian residents and from $29,800 to $46,000 for international students.
The numbers are similar at University of Ottawa, with Canadians paying $6,100-$6,700 and international students paying $43,300.
In 2022-23, non-Canadian students at Algonquin paid a premium ranging from $13,300 to $14,340.
The average tuition for non-Canadian undergrads in Ontario is $46,400, the highest of all provinces.
A huge gap also exists in post-graduate tuition costs.
At Carleton, Canadian students pay between $4,900 and $7,200 and non-Canadians pay between $13,000 and $17,700. Again, uOttawa numbers are comparable: $6,300-$8,600 for Canadians and $17,500-$31,500 for international students.
Graduate students paid an additional $11,100 premium at Algonquin in 2022-23.
Add it all up and foreign students are, in effect, subsidizing Canadian students.
According to a 2022 report by Ontario Attorney General Bonnie Lysyk, international students made up only 14% of the province's university population, but accounted for 45% of tuition revenues.
In that same report, Lysyk found that 68% of all college tuition fees in the province came from non-Canadian students.
Shutting down 'fake' schools Critics of the federal government's decision to cut visas say the issue isn't so much with legitimate, long-standing post-secondary institutions, but more about shady new private educational facilities that prey on desperate international students.
"We think a more refined, targeted approach would be better policy and not impact the good performers that rely on that funding or revenue from international schools to operate," Steve Orsini, president and CEO of the Council of Ontario Universities told The Canadian Press.
Orsini said the federal government should be more concerned with institutions that "have exponential growth in students that don't provide supports, the wraparound services or housing."
The government has, in the past, made a point of attacking those schools for exploitation.
"There are, in provinces, the diploma equivalent of puppy mills that are just churning out diplomas, and this is not a legitimate student experience," Miller said at a December media conference. "There is fraud and abuse and it needs to end."
International students who graduate in Canada can apply for work visas, which has given rise to an increase in unscrupulous schools and employers who take advantage of the situation.
In December, in an effort to curtail those situations, the federal government mandated that those applying for new student visas would need to prove they had access to at least $20,635 in funds beyond tuition. That would be more than double the previous requirement of $10,000 security to prove they could afford increased accommodation costs in Canada.
The federal government also says provincial governments have legislation in place to shut down institutions that operate against the spirit of the international student visa program.
"From my perspective, I don't take it personally," said Brulé of Algonquin College. "As a public institution, we deliver high-calibre education in our programming for students. We don't feel like we fall in that category. We stand by excellence."
Brulé and officials at uOttawa and Carleton don't know exactly how much the new government policy will impact their schools because they don't know yet how Ontario will choose to allocate the student visas provided by the federal government.
Faris, the Carleton mechanical engineering student, is fully engaged in the discussions.
"That increase in the required funds is going to limit the number of people who apply," he said. "It's not equitable for all (international) students. The criteria is based on a proof of having money and not based on academics." kwarren@postmedia.com

Subject: Foreign students; Corporate presidents; Passports & visas; Schools; College campuses; Tuition; Federal government; College presidents; Colleges & universities; Immigration; Mechanical engineering

Location: Canada; Dubai United Arab Emirates; United Arab Emirates

Document 439 of 453

Trudeau not doing little things to make life affordable

Author: Terrazzano, Franco

Publication info: Terrazzano, Franco. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-not-doing-little-things-make-life/docview/2918378332/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+not+doing+little+things+to+make+life+affordable&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.9&amp;au=Terrazzano%2C+Franco&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Figuring out how to make life more affordable for Canadians shouldn't be like unravelling Einstein's theory of relativity.
If Prime Minister Justin Trudeau won't do the big things to make life more affordable, he could at least do the little things.
"We know Canadians are facing challenging times right now, people are squeezed between the cost of groceries, rents," Trudeau said during a cabinet retreat in Montreal aimed at "bringing down the cost of living."
Trudeau knows "people are squeezed" because his tax hikes are some of the things that are doing the squeezing.
Take the carbon tax. Trudeau may never scrap his carbon tax, but he could at least not raise it again on April 1.
Even after the rebates, average families will be out hundreds of dollars this year because of the higher heating bills, gas prices, inflation and the economic damage wrought by the carbon tax, according to the parliamentary budget officer.
Governments of all political stripes have paused fuel taxes to provide relief.
British Columbia's New Democrats delayed their carbon tax hike during the pandemic.
Manitoba's NDP government suspended its fuel tax.
Newfoundland and Labrador's Liberals are also providing fuel tax relief, and so are the Conservative governments in Alberta and Ontario.
The United Kingdom, Sweden, Australia, South Korea, the Netherlands, Germany, Norway, India, Ireland, Israel, Italy, New Zealand and Portugal also provided fuel tax relief.
To add insult to injury, the feds charge their sales tax on top of the carbon tax. That's right. The federal government applies its sales tax after all the per-litre taxes are added.
This tax-on-tax is costing Canadians about $500 million this year, according to the PBO.
By the end of 2030, the GST on the carbon tax alone will have cost Canadians $6.2 billion.
Ending the tax-on-tax is a simple way to save Canadians billions when fuelling up or heating their homes.
Trudeau knows taxes make it more expensive to stay warm during the winter.
Otherwise, why would he have taken the carbon tax off home heating oil for three years? That political ploy was an attempt to help Atlantic Canadians amid tanking poll numbers in this typical Liberal stronghold.
However, 97% of Canadian families use other forms of energy to heat their homes. Trudeau should extend the relief he provided to Atlantic Canadians to everyone by taking the carbon tax off all forms of home heating. That would save the average family using natural gas about $1,100 over three years.
Trudeau can also give farmers relief and ease grocery prices by making sure the original Bill C-234 becomes law this year, which would remove the carbon tax from the natural gas and propane used on farms.
The House of Commons already passed this relief twice, but it still isn't law because of shenanigans in the Senate.
The carbon tax on natural gas and propane that's used to heat barns and dry grain will cost farmers $1 billion by 2030, according to the PBO.
By making it more expensive for farmers to grow food, the carbon tax makes it more expensive for Canadians to buy food.
There's one more easy way for Trudeau to provide relief: stop his upcoming 4.7% alcohol tax hike.
Last year, the feds capped the annual increase at 2%.
Trudeau shouldn't have hiked the tax at all, but the smaller increase reduced the tax burden by $100 million. At a time when both consumers and businesses are struggling, freezing the alcohol tax is the least Trudeau could do.
The Trudeau government doesn't need an expensive getaway in Montreal to figure out how to make life more affordable. There's a simple solution - stop taking so much money from Canadians.
Terrazzano is federal director of the Canadian Taxpayers Federation

Subject: Fuel taxes; Canadians; Relief provisions; Sales taxes; Carbon; Heating; Natural gas; Environmental tax; Tax increases

Business indexing term: Subject: Fuel taxes Relief provisions Sales taxes Environmental tax Tax increases

Location: Montreal Quebec Canada; Canada

Identifier / keyword: figuring; affordable; canadians; unravelling; theory; relativity

Document 440 of 453

'Failure of federalism'; Lawyer blasts use of Emergencies Act, says raucous protest could also have been ended using other laws

Author: Laucius, Joanne

Publication info: Laucius, Joanne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/failure-federalism-lawyer-blasts-use-emergencies/docview/2918377455/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=%27Failure+of+federalism%27%3B+Lawyer+blasts+use+of+Emergencies+Act%2C+says+raucous+protest+could+also+have+been+ended+using+other+laws&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-25&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.6&amp;au=Laucius%2C+Joanne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: The Emergencies Act invoked in response to the 2022 convoy protest was "unreasonable, unjustified" and violated the Charter of Rights and Freedoms, according to a federal court decision released Tuesday.
But the raucous protest could also have been ended using other laws, says the lawyer who filed for the injunction that silenced the truck horns for 10 days.
"The question comes down to what level of government really failed. It was clear it was the municipal and provincial levels of government. The federal government didn't have jurisdiction, and it all unfolded on their doorsteps," Paul Champ, an Ottawa human rights lawyer, said Wednesday.
"We should have applied more pressure on the province."
Civil liberties groups had argued that the Liberal federal government went beyond its powers, violated the Charter and was not justified in invoking the Emergencies Act. The Liberals argued they had reasonable grounds to believe the protests posed a threat to national security and could not be dealt with in any other way.
Federal Court Justice Richard Mosley found that the record did not support a conclusion that the convoy protest had created "a critical, urgent and temporary situation" that was national in scope and could not effectively be dealt with under any other law of Canada. Although the harm being caused to Canada's economy, trade and commerce was very real and concerning, it did not constitute threats or the use of serious violence to persons or property, he wrote.
However, Mosley also found that the events of the convoy had gone "beyond legitimate protest" and were an "unacceptable breakdown of public order."
Former Somerset ward councillor Catherine McKenney said she had thought long and hard about the convoy participants since the report from the Public Order Emergency Commission chaired by Commissioner Paul Rouleau was released in February 2023.
"The participants violated the rights of people to live within our community in safety," McKenney said Wednesday.
"The Ottawa Police Service should have done their job, the city should have taken responsibility for its residents. Almost immediately people were afraid to be out in public. I remember reaching out a few days later and asking: 'What's the plan?'I got no response," McKenney said.
"Had they taken responsibility, I don't believe that three weeks later we would have needed the Emergencies Act."
Michelle Hurtubise, executive director of the Centretown Community Health Centre, said people who lived in downtown Ottawa at the time of the protest were concerned about the lack of action at the time.
People reported being intimidated and harassed, she said. Residents who had Pride flags or Black Lives Matter flags had feces thrown at their doors and balconies, she said. The Centretown Community Health Centre had an evacuation plan in effect. She remembers wearing a mask on her way to a vaccination clinic for children and being bumped at least half a dozen times as she walked into the "red zone" of the protest.
"Working downtown with diesel truck fumes was toxic," Hurtubise said. "I agree with the right to peaceful protest. I don't believe that includes bringing in 18-wheelers."
The Federal Court decision minimized the trauma that people continue to live with, Hurtubise said.
"We were failed by municipal authorities, we were failed by provincial authorities, we were failed by federal authorities," she said. "It was not peaceful assembly and the tools that were available were not useful in this situation."
Mosley's ruling stemmed from four legal challenges by civil liberty groups, including the Canadian Civil Liberties Association and the Canadian Constitutional Foundation.
The groups argued the federal government exceeded its powers by invoking the Emergencies Act on Feb. 14, 2022, in response to ongoing convoy protest blockades in Ontario and Alberta.
"Emergency is not in the eye of the beholder. Emergency powers are necessary in extreme circumstances, but they are also dangerous to democracy. They should be used sparingly and carefully. They cannot be used even to address a massive and disruptive demonstration if that could have been dealt with through regular policing and laws," the CCLA said in a statement.
Rouleau's findings criticized the lack of communication between the federal and Ontario governments and the City of Ottawa, with all three levels of government expressing frustration. Ontario Premier Doug Ford argued that management of the convoy protest was a national issue, but the province is ultimately responsible for effective policing in Ottawa, Rouleau's report said.
Mosley's decision was not much different from Rouleau's conclusion, which was that it was "a failure of federalism," Champ said.

Subject: Federalism; Court decisions; Emergencies; National security; Evacuations & rescues; Civil liberties

Location: Canada

Document 441 of 453

Another fine mess the feds created

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/another-fine-mess-feds-created/docview/2917703945/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Another+fine+mess+the+feds+created&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-23&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.10&amp;au=&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Attempting to fix yet another problem its own policies created, the Trudeau government on Monday announced a two-year cap on international students, in a system Immigration Minister Marc Miller described last week as "out of control."
That raises the question of why the government didn't act before now.
"It's not the intention of this program to have sham commerce degrees or business degrees (from colleges) that are sitting on top of a massage parlour that someone doesn't even go to and then they come into the province and drive an Uber," Miller said Monday.
If things are that bad, why was it allowed to continue for so long? In addition, how could the Trudeau government not have anticipated the problems it would create by dramatically hiking its immigration targets for permanent residents, imposing no limit on the number of international students and temporary foreign workers, and overwhelming cities with asylum seekers? It ignored warnings from its public servants two years ago that such policies would contribute to Canada's housing, affordability and health-care crises, when it announced an "ambitious" plan to boost Canada's annual immigration targets to 465,000 permanent residents in 2023, 485,000 in 2024 and 500,000 in 2025.
It's since announced another 500,000 target for 2026, compared to 272,000 when the Liberals came to power in 2015.
There are currently 900,000 international students in Canada - permits last for three years at a time - compared to 352,325 in 2015 when the Liberals came to power.
So don't expect Monday's announcement to reduce the number of international student visas this year by 35% - to 364,000 compared to 560,000 last year - with the 2025 target to be set later this year, to perform miracles.
To be fair, not all of this is on the federal government. The provinces decide which schools are authorized to accept international students, which has led to situations where some schools are degree mills churning out useless diplomas as a way to short-circuit the normal process for being accepted into Canada from abroad.
Meanwhile, many universities have been admitting foreign students - who pay higher tuition costs - to improve their bottom lines. In 2020, for example, tuition paid by international students accounted for 70% of all tuition paid in Ontario.

Subject: Foreign students; Immigration

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: attempting; another; problem; policies; created; trudeau

Title: Another fine mess the feds created

Document 442 of 453

Notley exit will end banner days for Alberta NDP

Author: Gunter, Lorne

Publication info: Gunter, Lorne. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/notley-exit-will-end-banner-days-alberta-ndp/docview/2915816472/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Notley+exit+will+end+banner+days+for+Alberta+NDP&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Gunter%2C+Lorne&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Now that Rachel Notley has announced her resignation as leader of the Alberta NDP, expect her party to return to its traditional distant-second status.
Not as distant as it used to be, but not within sniffing distance of government again, either.
After 10 years as leader - four of them as premier - Notley leaves her party in better shape than she found it. In the nine elections before Notley took over the provincial NDP, the party had six leaders and won an average of just five seats per campaign.
In the three elections under her leadership, the Alberta NDP won an average of 39 seats.
During some fiscal quarters, they have even out-fundraised the United Conservative Party (UCP), an achievement that never happened before Notley.
I recall during the 2015 election (the one the NDP won) an organizer telling me the party had little idea where to focus its resources because it couldn't afford the kind of detailed, internal polling the UCP and its predecessor, the Progressive Conservatives, did routinely.
Those days of by-guess-and-bygolly campaigning are gone forever for the NDP, thanks to the way Notley succeeded in getting anti-UCP voters to support her party as the sole credible alternative on the left. (The Alberta Liberals, for instance, finished eighth in the May 2023 election with just 0.2% of the vote).
Still, five years from now the Notley days are likely to be seen as the glory days for the Alberta NDP. She is their biggest asset. Indeed, outside Edmonton, Notley is the party's only asset. Without her, I predict it will be impossible for the NDP to recapture government.
There are only two ways the NDP could win a general election.
They could face a divided right split between two equally credible conservative parties. That's how they won in 2015 when the Progressive Conservatives and the Wildrose carved up the right-of-centre vote and allowed the New Democrats to waltz up the middle.
Or they could win solid majorities of seats in Edmonton and Calgary.
The first scenario seems unlikely.
The right should remain united through the next election in 2027.
That leaves the NDP's only path to power, with or without Notley, a double win in Edmonton and Calgary.
While a remote-controlled traffic cone operating under the NDP banner could retain nearly every Edmonton seat for the NDP (the massive civil service and public-sector vote in the city gives the NDP an almost insurmountable head start), Calgary remains a largely small-c, pro-business city.
The NDP came close to winning enough Calgary seats to pull off an overall win last spring, but that had as much to do with Calgarians'uncertainty about then-new UCP Leader Danielle Smith as it did with Notley's popularity.
When Notley made a rash promise at mid-campaign to gouge businesses through higher taxes, enough Calgarians swallowed their reservations about Smith to keep the UCP comfortably in power.
Now add in two factors: By 2027, Smith will have been premier for nearly five years. Unless she goes suddenly extremist, most voters will have accepted Smith is qualified. At lot of the 80,000 to 100,000 UCP voters who stayed home during the last vote will return and swamp the NDP, especially in Calgary.
And the second factor to consider is that no one the NDP is likely to select to replace Notley will have her profile, her skills or her personality.
The default vote in Calgary and the rest of the province outside Edmonton remains UCP. It takes a special opposition leader (i.e. Rachel Notley) and special circumstances (a divided right) to overcome that default.
The NDP are unlikely to go back to five or fewer seats per election, but without Notley they are just as unlikely to win power. lgunter@postmedia.com @sunlornegunter

Subject: Leadership; Voters; Elections

Business indexing term: Subject: Leadership

Location: Canada; Calgary Alberta Canada

Document 443 of 453

Grim outlook; Hospitals desperate for funding, critics say

Author: Payne, Elizabeth

Publication info: Payne, Elizabeth. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/grim-outlook-hospitals-desperate-funding-critics/docview/2915816405/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Grim+outlook%3B+Hospitals+desperate+for+funding%2C+critics+say&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Payne%2C+Elizabeth&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Most are reluctant to speak publicly about the financial uncertainty they are facing right now, but behind closed doors many Ontario hospital officials are desperate, opposition health critics say.
"Many of them are worried about how they are supposed to continue their operations and respond at a time when they are facing unprecedented pressure," said Dr. Adil Shamji, who is the Ontario Liberal caucus critic for health.
Shamji said he was hearing about hospitals that were considering limiting surgeries amid financial insecurity and pressure from emergency department and hospital overcrowding.
"At a time when pressures in our emergency departments and our patient care are at their highest, hospitals need the funding in order to be able to respond and serve our communities. The big risk is that they do not have the capability and the funding in order to do so and at the end of the day, there are lives on the line."
He said the province's willingness to allow hospitals to "hang by a thread" was a damning indictment of their risk management and signalled their satisfaction with the status quo.
"The near universal state of hospitals running deficits in our province is frankly unprecedented," Shamji said.
This newspaper has reported that the vast majority of the 140 hospitals in Ontario are projecting deficits this year. In addition, some have taken out high-interest loans to keep going and others have had to dig into reserves earmarked for specific projects.
The Ontario government has issued special waivers to most hospitals allowing them to carry deficits, the Ontario Hospital Association says. Hospitals in the province are, under normal circumstances, required to balance their budgets and such waivers are rarely used.
"I have never seen it this bad," said France Gélinas, who is health critic with the Official Opposition NDP.
"I have had many CEOs call me directly and had long talks about how desperate they are, and so are their boards," she said.
The situation is particularly dire in the North and among rural hospitals, which increasingly rely on agency nurses to remain open - that costs the hospital significantly more than they pay regular staff.
At some locations in the North, nursing agency representatives sit in the parking lot and hire nurses as they are leaving their shifts at the hospital.
"The next day the nurse comes in at twice the wage. It is the same nurse that has been there for 10 years and now doesn't have to work night shifts or weekend and can pick and choose her shifts," Gélinas said.
It then becomes more difficult to fill those shifts and nursing agencies charge hospitals a premium to hire temporary nurses to do so, exacerbating a hospital's financial strain, she said.
Gélinas has tabled a bill calling for regulation of private nursing agencies.
A key issue for all hospitals is that the Ontario government has not fully reimbursed them for retroactive payments to hospital staff covered by the wage restraint legislation Bill 124, which was declared unconstitutional by the courts.
Last month the Ontario Hospital Association wrote to provincial officials urging them to fully reimburse hospitals for the retroactive payouts, but the OHA also said the worsening financial woes facing the province's hospitals go beyond those payments and reflect years of underfunding. Ontario has the fewest hospital beds per population among Canadian provinces and the lowest rate among countries followed by the Organization for Economic Cooperation and Development (OECD).
The discontinuation of special pandemic-related funding last year, even as Ontario is experiencing one of its biggest pandemic waves, has added more stress to hospitals. OHA president and CEO Anthony Dale said he thought that COVID-19 should continue to be a "fixed cost" for hospitals for the foreseeable future.
Shamji said he wanted to see the provincial government come forward with funding for the retroactive payments, to restore pandemic funding and to regulate temporary nursing agencies.

Subject: Emergency medical care; Nursing; Funding; Nurses; Pandemics; Critics

Company / organization: Name: Organization for Economic Cooperation & Development; NAICS: 541720

No 'blood money'; Paramount Foods CEO in hot seat after X post

Author: Warmington, Joe

Publication info: Warmington, Joe. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-blood-money-paramount-foods-ceo-hot-seat-after/docview/2915816383/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No+%27blood+money%27%3B+Paramount+Foods+CEO+in+hot+seat+after+X+post&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-17&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.8&amp;au=Warmington%2C+Joe&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Paramount Foods was in damage control following reaction Monday to a now-deleted tweet from founder Mohamad Fakih, suggesting he doesn't "need" money from someone who "supports baby killers."
The member of the Order of Canada and CEO of Paramount, which operates dozens of restaurants and has an arena in Mississauga named after the company, made the comments on the social media platform X. He was responding to a poster named Eugene who wrote, "I'll never set foot in Paramount Foods ever again" and "terrorist sympathizers don't deserve my business."
A respected philanthropist and fundraiser, Fakih - who has appeared in photographs with Prime Minister Justin Trudeau and Ontario Liberal Leader Bonnie Crombie - responded "we don't want your money. Please don't bring us blood money."
In an earlier X post, since deleted, he said: "we don't need your money" since "our clients are respectful families from all backgrounds ... we welcome everyone, but not the one that supports baby killers and government that (is) starving innocent civilians."
From there, Fakih said, people on X posted his picture with the tweet and created a false anti-Semitic narrative. Whatever his intention, the reaction was swift.
"With his social-media post, this Order of Canada recipient reminded many Canadians of the days when Jews were not welcome as patrons in various establishments throughout the country," said Richard Robertson, B'nai Brith Canada's manager of research.
"The hurt caused by his message was amplified by his usage of the phrase 'blood money,'a term reminiscent of the classic anti-Semitic trope of blood libel."
Robertson added: "it is difficult to fathom that such a highly regarded Canadian could make such a deeply offensive comment."
While B'nai Brith did not call for Fakih's Order of Canada to be revoked, others have. In an X post, PC MPP Lisa MacLeod said Fakih "must be either removed ... or resign."
When contacted for comment, Fakih - who this week has been leading a team providing hot meals to those on the streets - countered he was "shocked" by the harsh reaction, saying his posts were "misconstrued" and never intended to be taken as many did.
Fakih, who rose from humble beginnings to enjoy great success after coming to Canada from Lebanon in his 20s, told the Toronto Sun that "for the last 100 days," he never "said anything against the Jewish community" and that he "just wants things to calm down and be fair."
He said his staff received hateful comments that scared them, and people have been giving Paramount's restaurants scathing reviews on social media.
Fakih claimed he also got "death" threats. He said he was very clear in his posts that "everyone is welcome" and the "spin" presented by those reacting to the tweets was "inaccurate."
While Fakih was uncomfortable with the negative social media attention, he was part of the public campaign to see hockey legend Don Cherry lose his spot on Hockey Night in Canada after Grapes made his "you people" comment.
On what was then called Twitter, Fakih tweeted "instead of insulting those of us who have chosen Canada as the place we want to build our lives, perhaps Don Cherry should consider matching my commitment."
Now it's Fakih who faces public scrutiny.
While he insisted it's clear his posts were not anti-Semitic, Fakih acknowledged the damage was done. Others online contended he did the damage to himself.
Fakih said his comments were directed toward one person and not intended to go beyond that.
Some accept that explanation, and some do not.
That said, Fakih has been staunchly critical of Israel in the war against Hamas. On Jan. 11, he responded to an X post by commentator Avi Lewis, who was arguing that Israel had a responsibly to defend itself from terror.
Fakih's responded, "a legal absurdity to call this genocide? It's a moral absurdity to call it anything else."
When challenged that he does not have a robust track record of publicly denouncing Hamas, whose militants slaughtered 1,200 Jews and kidnapped more than 200 people on Oct. 7, he acknowledged they are considered a terrorist group and he does not support them.
Fakih recently posted surprising criticism of the Trudeau government, which he routinely supports. Trudeau even posted a video to congratulate Paramount on its 10th anniversary.
But Fakih spoke out against the Trudeau government's decision not to support the South African charges before the International Court of Justice in The Hague, alleging Israel had committed war crimes in its execution of the war in Gaza.
"I am a Liberal. This crew has lost its way," Fakih posted on X. jwarmington@postmedia.com @joe_warmington

Document 445 of 453

No, you're not getting all your money back

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/no-youre-not-getting-all-your-money-back/docview/2915440780/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=No%2C+you%27re+not+getting+all+your+money+back&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Did you get money from the federal government this week? I got $183 to compensate me for the money I spent due to the carbon tax.
All in all, not a good bargain. Sure, I'd rather have the money than not. I am not giving it back. But better still would be if we didn't have the carbon tax in the first place.
All the arguments for it fail the logic test.
The stated purpose is to incent us to spend less on carbon fuels by charging more for them. That's solid economics as far as it goes. Generally as price goes up, demand goes down.
But they promised to give us the money back. Why should I buy less gasoline if the price is neutral or negligible? As such it was a failed proposition from the get-go.
Of course most people won't get all their money back. That is a lie.
The Parliamentary Budget Office, calculating the negative effect the tax has on the economy says, "Most households in Alberta, Saskatchewan, Manitoba and Ontario will see a net loss resulting from federal carbon pricing. That is, the costs they face including the federal carbon levy, higher GST and lower incomes will exceed the Climate Action Incentive rebate they receive."
By implanting and steadily increasing the tax, the government is punishing Canadians for something we are not responsible for.
50% of the carbon emitted on the planet is from three nations combined; China, India and the USA.
Canadians account for less than two percent.
Fanatics will jump up to say, "But we are among the highest emitters per capita."
That is irrelevant. It is estimated that if we emitted nothing we would account for a drop of one quarter of one percent in worldwide emissions.
To put that in perspective let's imagine 400 cows in a closed building.
With all the methane pouring out of cows, it would get pretty close in there.
So, let's remove a cow. Anyone going to notice a difference if the remaining 99 keep on blowing? Of course not. We are the one cow, not the 99.
Ontario closed five coal burning plants to serve a green agenda. China builds two a week, so again we made no difference.
Another reason people give for adopting green policies and taxes whether a small country like ours makes a difference in emissions is that they say we have to be an example to the world.
Really? Name a country that cares what we do.
Does anyone believe a meeting ever took place in China where someone said, "Canada is closing coal plants and taxing their citizens. We better slow it down and go green, or we'll look bad to Canadian liberals."
People will call my radio show and say, "We have to do something."
No we don't. Just doing something just to be doing something is juvenile at best and really, just dumb.
To solve any problem it is necessary to do something effective, but as pointed out, there is nothing effective about the carbon tax.
The only winners are federal bureaucrats cozily employed to administer the program and send us money to try to hoodwink us into believing we are getting a good deal.
Federal employees have great benefits, none of which accrues to the rest of us, or the planet.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca

Subject: Federal employees; Carbon; Environmental tax

Business indexing term: Subject: Environmental tax

Location: China

Identifier / keyword: money; federal; government; compensate; spent; carbon

Document 446 of 453

Strike's on; PSAC seeking deal for Non-Public Funds workers

Author: Morrison, Carine

Publication info: Morrison, Carine. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/strikes-on-psac-seeking-deal-non-public-funds/docview/2915440748/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Strike%27s+on%3B+PSAC+seeking+deal+for+Non-Public+Funds+workers&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-16&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.3&amp;au=Morrison%2C+Carine&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Almost 500 Non-Public Funds workers represented by the Public Service Alliance of Canada (PSAC) made their way to picket lines in Ontario and Quebec on Monday morning to demand a fair contract agreement.
Members working in Ottawa rallied together by the Canadian Forces Morale and Welfare Ser vices (CFMWS) and the CANEX Retail Store buildings on Labelle Street starting at 8 a.m. Monday morning. Pickets were bundled in winter jackets, hats and ski pants as the windchill made if feel like -20 C.
According to PSAC, strike action is impacting Non-Public Funds workers in Bagotville, Kingston, Saint-Jean-sur-Richelieu, Ottawa, Petawawa and Valcartier. Some members made their way to picket lines by 6:30 a.m.
Julie Laska, picket captain and president of Local 70682 under the Union of National Defence Employees, said she has worked as a Non-Public Funds employee for almost eight years. She said it's bittersweet to be on strike, but said workers need to be on the line to fight for their salaries.
"Do we want to be here? No. We want to reach an agreement with the employer," said Laska, who is a member of the bargaining team. "People are here. We're cold, but we're here. It's important."
CFMWS is a separate agency, with NPF workers supporting Canadian Armed Forces members, veterans and their families by delivering morale and various welfare services. Forming part of the federal public service, they work in retail and food service through the Canadian Forces Exchange System, in financial planning for Canadian Armed Forces members through SISIP financial and in community service, physical fitness and recreation.
According to the Government of Canada website, CFMWS operates on behalf of the chief of defence staff and under the authority of the defence minister. As staff are not National Defence or Treasury Board employees, they have their own pension and pay system, and collective agreements.
Without a contract since 2022, members took part in strike votes from Nov. 20 to Dec. 14. On average, 94 per cent of members voted in favour of strike action. This is the first time that Non-Public Funds workers have voted for a strike mandate and the first PSAC strike since the national movement last spring.
NPF workers are fighting for fair wages, a national pay grid for workers across Canada and better job security. According to PSAC, NPF workers in Petawawa make less than a dollar per hour more than the federal minimum wage.
"We are some of the lowest-paid public servants," Laska said, adding that about 75 members are working in Ottawa, though low wages cause a high rate of turnaround. "I love that I get to support the military every single day, and I just want to be supported to do that role."
PSAC national president Chris Aylward said this strike is about wages. While the parties are "not that far apart," he said they're still "not there."
"You could be doing one job here in Ottawa and somebody in Edmonton could be doing the exact same job but yet get paid six or seven dollars more an hour," Aylward said.
"For far too long, these members have been underpaid, and now we're looking basically to do catch up. It's almost like a domino effect. You pay the workers a low wage, they move on and the military are the ones who suffer because there's a constant changeover and turnover in the staff who provide those services."
Alex Silas, PSAC's regional executive vice-president for the National Capital Region, said last week that the agency was using "scare tactics," encouraging workers to cross the picket line, signalling that their pay would be stopped and that benefits would be cut off.
In a statement, NDP labour critic Alexandre Boulerice said it was "deeply disappointing that the federal government has been unable to reach a fair agreement" with PSAC.
"These dedicated workers answer calls from our Canadian Forces seeking crucial support for their well-being and for morale," Boulerice said.
"We call on the Liberals not to interfere in these workers'right to strike, and to swiftly come to the negotiation table with a fair agreement that respects these workers who serve to ensure that Canada's forces and their families are provided with the services and programs they need."
Andrée-Anne Poulin, a spokesperson for the Department of National Defence, said the CFMWS is "prepared for strike action."
"CFMWS services that could be impacted include, but are not limited to, physical fitness facilities, CANEX (military retail store), and recreation programs," Poulin said. "CFMWS aims to maintain the same level of service and programming so we can continue to serve our members despite the strikes."
Poulin said operational committees are in place to support the organization during a strike and ensure "minimal disruptions" to key operations as well as the protection of personnel, property and facilities. Poulin said plans are in place to continue providing "certain services," adding that the impact on other services is being mitigated by reducing facility opening hours and rescheduling non-essential events.
"CFMWS regrets that an agreement could not be reached between the parties to date," Poulin said. "We are hopeful that the parties can reach a negotiated agreement at the table, and remain dedicated to the collective bargaining efforts aimed at achieving a fair contract for our employees."
Diana Ebadi, press secretary to Minister of National Defence Bill Blair, said the minister believes that the best deals are made at the table and encourages both PSAC and CFMWS to work together on achieving a fair contract for employees.

Subject: Agreements; Physical fitness; Strikes; Defense; Collective bargaining; Employees; Armed forces; Wages & salaries

Document 447 of 453

A new gov't staple; Expanding ServiceOntario into retail hardly cause for 'serious concerns'

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/new-govt-staple-expanding-serviceontario-into/docview/2914972024/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=A+new+gov%27t+staple%3B+Expanding+ServiceOntario+into+retail+hardly+cause+for+%27serious+concerns%27&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-15&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.4&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Progressives at Queen's Park sure don't seem to like progress. This week, the so-called progressive parties at Queen's Park are protesting that the Doug Ford government wants to move some ServiceOntario locations into Staples stores in southern Ontario.
The move was announced in December by a government news release, but became something for the opposition parties to start complaining about this week after a CityNews story.
NDP Leader Marit Stiles is worried that the government is working with a "U.S.-owned corporation like Staples" to deliver services. Liberal MP Adil Shamji is worried about the "corporatizing" of government services.
"This raises serious concerns about Ontarians'access to government services," NDP critic Catherine Fife said.
Really? This move to work with Staples includes about 11 locations across the province. There are close to 300 ServiceOntario locations and almost three-quarters of them are operated through private delivery.
Shamji, who ran for the Ontario Liberal leadership before endorsing Bonnie Crombie, should know this as it was his party that greatly expanded the private sector's involvement during the Dalton McGuinty and Kathleen Wynne years.
"In what world does it make sense to go to an office supply chain to renew your driver's licence or health card?" Green party Leader Mike Schreiner asked.
Maybe about as much sense as it makes to renew your driver's licence and health card at a Canadian Tire store. The last time I had to renew both of those documents, I went to the Canadian Tire on Yonge St. just north of Bloor St. in Toronto, one of the closest locations to Queen's Park, and the service was just fine.
Convenience, ease of access The people complaining about this really don't seem to know what they are talking about when it comes to service delivery. They also seem like the kind of people who would like to go back to the good old days of having to search out dingy government offices with horrible hours to access services.
Would anyone really like to go back to the days when, to access Canada Post, you had to find an official post office location and get there before 5 p.m.? Or do you enjoy the convenience of going to your local Shoppers to get stamps, mail a package or pick up that delivery you missed while you were at work? The devil will be in the details and the execution, but having visited Staples of late this is a service that will blend in perfectly with their new format.
The company is moving away from just being a place to buy office paper at a price cheaper than Dunder Mifflin and branching out into other service areas. The politicians complaining at Queen's Park would be wise to walk down University Ave. to the local Staples and see the co-working space, the coffee shop and the shipping kiosk serving FedEx, Purolator and more before dismissing the idea of a ServiceOntario kiosk.
What we should be looking for are more innovative ways to deliver services in ways that are convenient to residents. In November, the province announced a hub with the Township of Pickle Lake to deliver municipal and provincial services at the same location, similar to Ottawa City Hall, where you can access services from all three levels of government at one convenient place.
While this is being described as a "sole-sourced contract" - rarely a good move for any government - the truth is the province is looking to work with other partners as well and Staples was the first to strike a deal. Look for other changes in service delivery, including a greater ability to access services online, to come in the future.
While opposition politicians will grumble, most of us will just be thankful for the expanded access. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Political leadership

Company / organization: Name: ServiceOntario; NAICS: 926120

Identifier / keyword: progressives; progress; progressive; parties; protesting; government

Document 448 of 453

Trudeau government's promises of deficit reduction ring hollow

Author: Eisen, Ben; Fuss, Jake

Publication info: Eisen, Ben; Fuss, Jake. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeau-governments-promises-deficit-reduction/docview/2913811931/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau+government%27s+promises+of+deficit+reduction+ring+hollow&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-13&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.15&amp;au=Eisen%2C+Ben%3BFuss%2C+Jake&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: As budget season approaches, it's worth remembering that policymakers must pair promises of fiscal discipline with concrete action.
According to projections, the Trudeau government will run a $40-billion deficit this fiscal year, which is slightly larger than last year's $35.3-billion deficit, even though revenues are up almost $10 billion.
Finance Minister Chrystia Freeland has repeatedly said the era of large deficits won't last forever and promised that starting in 2026-2027 the federal deficit will be held every year to less than 1% of Gross Domestic Product (the value of all goods and services produced in the economy).
But Freeland's promises should be taken with more than a grain of salt because all governments have spotty track records when it comes to fiscal promises, particularly when they're based on spending restraint in the future. Conversely, the most credible promises are introduced with immediate action.
The most famous example in Canada's recent fiscal history came in the 1990s when Paul Martin, then the Liberal government's finance minister, vowed to slay Canada's crippling budget deficit and repair federal finances "come hell or high water." Martin's words were accompanied by immediate action in the form of the transformative 1995 federal budget that began a process of deep budget cuts and major policy reforms that immediately put the government on a path to fiscal balance. The deficit was quickly eliminated and Canada was put on a sound fiscal footing that served the country well for almost two decades.
The "hell or high water" episode shows us how governments must match strong commitments with strong actions to increase the likelihood of success. On the other hand, promises for fiscal improvements in the future - unaccompanied by any concurrent action - usually aren't worth the paper (or tweets) they're printed on.
The Trudeau government has produced many examples of incredible promises. First, on the campaign trail back in 2015, Trudeau and his team promised small and temporary deficits and to shrink the national debt burden. Since then, the prime minister and his finance ministers have never been shy about promising to improve the bottom line. However, the target dates associated with these promises came and went without much notice and the government pivoted to new plausible-sounding targets, using only words without policy change.
Again, the Trudeau government is far from alone in this tendency. In Ontario, former premier Kathleen Wynne's government repeatedly promised to shrink that province's daunting debt burden, yet failed to decisively act. Basically, it was wishful thinking.
After Ontarians voted out the Wynne government, the Conservative Doug Ford government promised to break from the Wynne approach and introduce real reforms that would slay the deficit quickly. But after a few half-hearted feints in the direction of fiscal discipline, the Ford government also failed to make good on its promises of debt reduction and as a result, Ontario remains stuck under a mountain of government debt.
Canadian history is clear - unless government promises coincide with concrete actions to immediately start shrinking budget deficits, these promises of fiscal restraint at some future date shouldn't be given much credence. Freeland's recent promises fall into this category. In the upcoming federal budget, if the Trudeau government and its cabinet want its promises to be credible, it must learn from its Liberal predecessors and the "hell or high water" moment and reduce spending to immediately begin a process of deficit reduction.
Ben Eisen and Jake Fuss are analysts at the Fraser Institute

Subject: National debt; Decades; Debt restructuring; Budget deficits

Business indexing term: Subject: National debt Debt restructuring Budget deficits

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: budget; season; approaches; worth; remembering; policymakers

YOU SAID IT: Guilbeault OK with it?

Author: Menary, Doug

Publication info: Menary, Doug. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/blogs-podcasts-websites/you-said-guilbeault-ok-with/docview/2913642708/se-2?accountid=15090

Full text: GUILBEAULT OK WITH IT?
It appears that even the environment minister's eco-evangelism has its limits. In attempting to defend the prime minister's latest winter getaway, Steven Guilbeault claimed that the PM could have "gone to a cottage in the Laurentians, and people would have found a way to criticize his vacation."
Perhaps, but any true environmentalist would have acknowledged that a long-haul flight with a large entourage produces far more CO2 emissions than a quick jaunt to Harrington Lake.
However, beleaguered taxpayers will again pick up the tab for the offsets to make sure the PM travelled in luxurious "carbon neutral" carriage. This is the world we live in, with this king, and his climate change court jester.
AL WILLEY
EDMONTON
SPEEDING INTO EV MARKET
Re: Honda mulls $18.4B domestic EV plant, Jan. 8
Germany's Volkswagen was given $16 billion by Justin Trudeau and Doug Ford to build electric batteries in Ontario. Now Japan's Honda wants in on the action. This is all well and good, except these politicians have not said where they plan to obtain all the additional electricity needed to fuel these EVs. Also, where is all the new needed infrastructure? They are putting the cart before the horse!
To make matters worse, U.S. Ford Corp. and other big U.S. EV manufacturers are losing billions because nobody is buying these vehicles. Thousands of workers who manufacture EVs in the U.S. are currently being laid off.
It seems Trudeau is blindly rushing into an EV market based on his ideology, not sound marketing research. Because of the exorbitant purchase cost of EVs, even with taxpayer-funded subsidies, purchases of EVs will remain slow.
LARRY COMEAU
OTTAWA
IN A PIG'S EYE
Re: REPLACING THE ALEXANDRA: Ottawa's new bridge is challenged with the heavy weight of symbolism, online, Jan. 6
Just build the damn thing - the Alexandra Bridge or whatever they may choose to name it. It's typical of the government, especially our current federal one, to literally study naming it and dedications to just about everyone and their brother before they start construction.
Now the tear-down date is 2028, maybe, but the completion date is 2032, Yeah, in a pig's eye.
STEPHEN FLANAGAN
OTTAWA
GO SHOW THE WAY, JUSTIN
Do you think Justin Trudeau should go to Germany to show its government how to handle protesters? After all, he has experience with it. He may need another break from his responsibilities.
ALLAN SEMENCHUK
EDMONTON

Document 451 of 453

Trudeau's support evaporating among key demographic groups

Author: Lilley, Brian

Publication info: Lilley, Brian. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/trudeaus-support-evaporating-among-key/docview/2913595243/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Trudeau%27s+support+evaporating+among+key+demographic+groups&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-12&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Lilley%2C+Brian&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: You have to wonder how low Justin Trudeau's support would be if it weren't for seniors. Right now, that's the best performing demographic for voter support that Trudeau's Liberals can count on and he's not even winning them.
The latest poll from Abacus Data shows Pierre Poilievre and the Conservatives with a 17-point lead over Trudeau's Liberals. The Conservatives lead among every demographic group and in every region except Quebec, while the Liberals don't have a lead anywhere to be found.
Nationally, the Conservatives have the support of 41% of voters, the Liberals are in second with 24% support and the NDP in third at 18%.
It's the regional and demographic numbers that tell the real story.
In Atlantic Canada, 41% of voters in this former Liberal stronghold now say they will vote Team Blue with just 31% saying they still back the Team Red. The Red Wall has not only crumbled in Atlantic Canada - in Ontario, where the Liberals currently hold 78 seats, the Conservatives lead 41% to 29%.
In those two regions alone there would be a massive change in seat counts if an election were held today.
It doesn't really matter that the Conservatives have 57% support across Saskatchewan because they already hold every seat but the four Liberals in Manitoba and two in Alberta need to be worried about their future as Tory numbers rise.
In British Columbia, the Conservatives are taking 43% support to 19% for the Liberals and 26% for the NDP. B.C. has often been a tight three-way race over the last several election cycles but, right now, the Liberals are collapsing, and the Conservatives are benefiting.
Demographic trends are shifting Perhaps what has to be scariest for the Liberals is that their share of support among women and other demographic groups has evaporated and, in some instances, they are tied with the NDP or, even worse, in third place.
Just 25% of women say they will vote for Trudeau and the Liberals, a category they used to carry. Now, it's Poilievre taking the plurality of women voters with 37% saying they will back the Conservatives compared to 24% for the NDP.
Among those age 18-29, a demographic group the Liberals and NDP used to fight over, the Conservatives are again in the lead with 32% support compared to 28% for the NDP and 18% for the Liberals. This is likely the result of the housing affordability crisis, which Trudeau ignored for too long while Poilievre stepped forward to offer solutions.
The Conservatives have been ahead in the polls for months now, some polls giving them as high as a 19-point lead such as the Nanos poll released at the end of November. In mid-December, Abacus reported the Conservatives had dropped 5 points down to 37% support while the Liberals were up at 27%.
Many saw this as a slide in support for Poilievre, perhaps that he had peaked and was coming down. Abacus CEO David Coletto said it looks like any slide has stopped, even reversed.
"Since our last survey, the Conservatives are up 4 while the Liberals are down 3. This is a statistically significant shift in vote intentions since mid-December," Coletto said.
It's also a bad start to the year for the Liberals, who had hoped to turn things around in 2024.
This doesn't mean it's too late for them, it's just a bad sign of a continuing trend that will become harder to reverse as time goes on. As for the Conservatives, they shouldn't start popping champagne bottles just yet, we could still be a long way off from an election.
The rules to remember in politics: Polls change, voters are fickle, campaigns matter.
For now, though, it must feel pretty good to be Pierre Poilievre and pretty dismal for Justin Trudeau. blilley@postmedia.com @brianlilley

Subject: Polls & surveys; Voters

Location: Canada

People: Poilievre, Pierre; Trudeau, Justin

Identifier / keyword: wonder; justin; support; seniors; right; performing

Document 452 of 453

Apples to apples; Complaining about protesters not a two-way street

Author: Kinsella, Warren

Publication info: Kinsella, Warren. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/apples-complaining-about-protesters-not-two-way/docview/2910040219/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Apples+to+apples%3B+Complaining+about+protesters+not+a+two-way+street&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-04&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.18&amp;au=Kinsella%2C+Warren&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: Sorry, partisans, but you can't have it both ways.
January 2022: Protesters start blocking Ottawa streets to make a political point. Police are ultimately used to clear them out. Some conservatives are very unhappy the police used force to do so, while some progressives are happy.
January 2024: Protesters start blocking Toronto streets to make a political point. Police haven't cleared them out using force (yet). But some conservatives are very unhappy police haven't used force (yet) - while some progressives are happy they haven't.
I know, I know. Both sides are mad at me, now. Sorry. But you can't oppose (or favour) clearing out the Ottawa road occupiers, and now take the completely opposite position with the pro-Palestine road occupiers. It's illogical, it's inconsistent, and it's unfair.
Two years ago, this writer strongly favoured the police clearing out the Ottawa occupiers, with force if necessary. As I wrote in these pages at the time: "Enough is enough. It's time to invoke the Emergencies Act ... The Act would give the government the ability to prevent more troublemakers from travelling to Ottawa to extend the siege there. It would prevent 'protesters'from blocking border crossings - including, as they did in Windsor, with children.
"It would allow the authorities to remove trucks and barricades that have been used to shut down the national capital - and cripple billions of dollars in cross-border trade. And it allows government to compensate citizens and businesses who have been victimized by the lawlessness."
And, two years later, just about every word of that also applies to the "pro-Palestine" (read: kind-of, sort-of pro-Hamas) protesters, who are now regularly blocking access to and from Toronto's 401, the busiest highway in North America. Not only are they hurting local businesses and residents - like the Ottawa occupiers did - they are using their presence to intimidate the people (mainly Jews) who live in those areas.
What's fair is fair, what's good for the goose is good for the gander and any other cliché that applies. You simply can't be OK with one group blocking roads and intimidating locals, and then oppose it when another group does it. The law, if it is to matter at all, has to be applied without fear or favour. It has to be equitable.
At just about this point in this opinion column, or course, partisans on the Left and the Right are steaming mad, and want to argue the two situations are completely, totally different.
Right about now, they're dreaming up distinctions they hope make a difference.
But they are distinctions which don't amount to a difference. In international law, blockades are considered acts of war.
In statute law, it is also illegal: In Alberta, for example, the Critical Infrastructure Defence Act properly makes it against the law for "blockades, protests or similar activities" to damage or harm essential infrastructure like roads.
And in Ontario, during the Ottawa occupation, Premier Doug Ford (rightly) applied the provincial equivalent of the Emergencies Act days before Prime Minister Justin Trudeau did so to stop blockades of roads and border crossings. Meanwhile, Canada's Criminal Code's section 423 makes blocking roads a crime punishable by five years in prison.
For centuries, the non-criminal common law has done likewise: going back to the Seventies, the Supreme Court of Canada has held that "authorities were not only entitled but duty bound, as peace officers ... the right of free access of the public to public streets."
In international law, in Canada's criminal law, in our statutory laws, in centuries of common law, it's the iron-clad rule: you are not allowed to block public roads and highways with impunity. And the police have always had the authority to arrest and detain you for doing so.
So, in case of the Ottawa occupation and the "pro-Palestine" occupations, the law must be applied consistently and fairly. If it was right and proper to arrest and detain the Ottawa occupiers who refused to leave that city's streets, it's now right and proper to arrest and detain the pro-Palestine/anti-Semitic occupiers who are refusing to do likewise on the 401's exit ramps.
The law is the law. If you apply it in one case, you need to apply it in all similar cases.
So, why haven't the police arrested and cleared out the anti-Israel mobs? For that question, I do not have an answer.

Subject: International law; Roads & highways; Criminal law

Location: Canada

Identifier / keyword: sorry; partisans; january; protesters; start; blocking

Title: Apples to apples; Complaining about protesters not a two-way street

Document 453 of 453

Ontario NDP's spending plans ignore fiscal realities

Author: Agar, Jerry

Publication info: Agar, Jerry. 

https://libproxy.wlu.ca/login?url=https://www.proquest.com/newspapers/ontario-ndps-spending-plans-ignore-fiscal/docview/2909348114/se-2?accountid=15090

Abstract: None available.

Links: https://ocul-wlu.primo.exlibrisgroup.com/discovery/openurl?institution=01OCUL_WLU&amp;vid=01OCUL_WLU:WLU_DEF&amp;?url_ver=Z39.88-2004&amp;rft_val_fmt=info:ofi/fmt:kev:mtx:journal&amp;genre=article&amp;sid=ProQ:ProQ%3Acanadiannews&amp;atitle=Ontario+NDP%27s+spending+plans+ignore+fiscal+realities&amp;title=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;issn=08432570&amp;date=2024-01-03&amp;volume=&amp;issue=&amp;spage=A.11&amp;au=Agar%2C+Jerry&amp;isbn=&amp;jtitle=The+Ottawa+Sun&amp;btitle=&amp;rft_id=info:eric/&amp;rft_id=info:doi/

Full text: When the NDP says it will make life more affordable, questions have to be asked about how and for whom? Ontario NDP Leader Marit Styles released her five-part plan in the Toronto Star.
She promises to make housing more affordable, something everyone agrees is a good goal. How? "We need to bring back real rent control," she wrote.
Rent control, as with many political positions, is populist, but is it a good idea? No. It ignores the reality of the market. Prices rise because the amount demanded exceeds the amount supplied at existing prices.
Prices have a role to play, signalling when supply and demand are not in line. Price controls are artificial and ignore cost.
Price controls serve as a disincentive to developers to build and to landlords to spend on upkeep. Rent controls in New York have led to more rundown buildings, which is not helpful to low-income people.
Stiles writes, "Decades of reliance on the market alone has only made Ontario's housing crisis worse."
Is she suggesting housing problems can be solved by government? How well is that going with social housing? The worst landlord in Toronto is the City of Toronto, with $1.7 billion in backlogged social housing repairs.
Stiles'next point is to champion $10-a-day daycare, in part she says by "raising the wages of early childhood educators and other childcare staff."
It is a popular program idea among those who believe cheap daycare will allow more parents, mostly women, to work - which they say will help the economy - and those who quite frankly just want someone else to pay their bills.
Again, price has a role to play.
Artificially lowering the price while increasing the cost sets up a dynamic that will see some people luck out (or use their connections) while others will sit on long waiting lists.
How will supply match demand when almost all parents will see daycare as "affordable?" Canada already has some of the longest waiting times in the developed world for health care due to "free" universal care.
Adding dental and daycare to free or cheap services offered can only be achieved with massive tax increases, or as in health care, rationing through waiting lists, or both.
Stiles says she can make home heating more affordable. How? "By making heat pumps free."
Nothing is free. Someone pays. She will make groceries more affordable by "creating an independent consumer watchdog to prevent price gouging, investigate and fine corporate predators."
The profit margin on groceries is in the neighbourhood of 3%. So, if we reduce their profit by 33%, that will take a penny off a dollar of your grocery bill. Will you notice? Most profit for companies like Loblaw, studies show, comes from drug and finance operations.
The NDP's fifth platform is to double ODSP and OW payments to, it says, over 900,000 people.
Perhaps those programs should increase at a higher rate than the Conservatives' 5%.
Can Stiles tell us where she will find the money? Can she name any program spending she would cut in favour of ODSP and OW? Anyone can spend money. Not everyone can manage money or an economy.
Once again, the NDP is delivering populist rhetoric, yelling "gouging" and "predator," while its knowledge of business and economics is as suspect as it has always been.
Agar is the 9 a.m. to noon host on NEWSTALK1010 jerry.agar@sunmedia.ca
